paper_title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Understanding how complex cognitive functions are organized within artificial systems is central to interpreting large language models (LLMs) and relating them to biological cognition. Yet although LLMs exhibit broad cognitive-like behaviours, it remains unclear whether their internal representations form reproducible functional systems that explain behaviour, failure and links to human cognition. Here we present NeuroCogMap, a cognitive neuroscience-inspired framework that organizes internal features of LLMs into functional parcels and links them to interpretable functions, cognitive capabilities and a cognitive hierarchy. These parcels form a stable and semantically coherent organization that is partly conserved across models and functionally linked to model outputs. Within this organization, major LLM failures, including hallucination, bias, refusal failure and sycophancy, correspond to distinct disruptions in representational and behavioural-control systems, yielding internal signatures for mechanism-guided detection and targeted intervention. Beyond model behaviour, NeuroCogMap improves prediction of human cortical responses during naturalistic language comprehension, with the strongest correspondence in higher-order association cortex. At the cognitive level, its internal signatures expose latent strategies that guide refinements of classical models of human decision-making. Together, these findings establish NeuroCogMap as a system-level framework for mapping functional organization in artificial systems and for relating this organization to human cortical function and cognitive behaviour.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Large language models (LLMs) have intensified the reciprocal relationship between cognitive neuroscience and artificial intelligence [1, 2, 3]. They exhibit broad cognitive-like behaviours while providing experimentally accessible systems in which complex functions arise within single trained architecture [4, 5]. Yet these capacities emerge without explicit modular design, pre-specified cognitive architecture or task-specific decomposition, leaving open fundamental systems question: how is cognitive organization instantiated within LLMs? Understanding this organization is essential for explaining how model capabilities arise, diagnosing the internal basis of systematic failures, and establishing whether artificial cognitive structure can provide testable insights into biological cognition. Current approaches provide only partial access to this question. Behavioural evaluation reveals what models can do, but not how distinct cognitive functions are implemented, coordinated or controlled internally [6, 7, 8]. Mechanistic studies have identified neurons, circuits and sparse features associated with particular computations, whereas representation-level analyses have revealed broader semantic structure [9, 10, 11, 12, 13]. These approaches nevertheless tend to operate either at local mechanistic scale or at broad representational scale. What remains missing is system-level account of how distributed components form coherent functional units, interact across levels of abstraction, support higher-order capabilities and break down during pathological behaviour. similar explanatory challenge long shaped cognitive neuroscience. sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: Figure 1 NeuroCogMap: cognitive neuroscience-inspired framework for mapping cognitive structure in large language models. a, Conceptual motivation. Human cognitive processes and neural activations are shown alongside cognitive capabilities and internal activations in LLMs, motivating shared framework for analysing cognitive organization across biological and artificial systems. b,c, Framework construction. NeuroCogMap begins by collecting broad capability space and associated evaluation datasets from the literature (b). Sparse model features are then extracted from LLMs and used to identify parcels that define functional atlas, which is linked to cognitive atlas of functions and further organized into cognitive capabilities (c). d, Internal structure of NeuroCogMap. NeuroCogMap provides multi-level description of cognition inside LLMs, linking parcel-level features and the structural connectome to the functional atlas, cognitive atlas, cognitive capabilities and the final cognitive hierarchy. Example parceland capability-level annotations are shown. eg, From LLM organization to human cognition. NeuroCogMap supports analysis of pathological model behaviours and their attribution, detection and mitigation (e), establishes neural-level link to human cortical function by linking LLM parcels to human neural activity (f), and guides cognitively grounded refinement of classical cognitive models (g). principles that linked distributed brain activity to functional specialization [14, 15]. Functional parcellation, representational mapping, hierarchical abstraction and pathology-based inference together established the human brain as structured cognitive system composed of interacting subsystems [16, 15, 17, 18, 19, 20]. These same principles suggest path forward for LLMs, while the models themselves offer an experimental advantage unavailable in biology: their internal states can be observed, perturbed and replicated at scale [21, 22]. This creates an opportunity not only to interpret LLMs more deeply, but also to study cognitive organization itself under conditions of systematic observation and intervention. These considerations motivate framework that treats LLM cognition as an organized internal system rather than as collection of isolated behaviours or mechanisms. Here we introduce NeuroCogMap, cognitive neuroscience-inspired framework for mapping cognitive structure within LLMs. NeuroCogMap begins by identifying coherent internal parcels from sparse model features, analogous to functional parcellation in the brain. It then links these parcels to an interpretable cognitive atlas of functions and capabilities, and further organizes them into hierarchical architecture spanning perception, representation, abstraction and application (Fig. 1b,c). In this way, NeuroCogMap provides multi-level description of cognition inside LLMs, from localized functional units to higher-order cognitive organization (Fig. 1d). We then asked whether NeuroCogMap could analyse systematic model failures, support comparison with human cortical organization and guide cognitive-model discovery. First, pathology analyses showed that representational and behavioural-control pathologies exhibited dissociable patterns of disruption across circuits, parcels, capabilities and hierarchy, and that these pathology signatures enabled detection and mechanismguided mitigation (Fig. 1e). Second, human-alignment analyses showed that NeuroCogMap parcel activity improved cortical-response prediction during naturalistic language comprehension relative to conventional model representations, with the most strongly aligned parcels showing functional correspondence with higherorder cortical systems in both functional profile and semantic description (Fig. 1f). Finally, cognitive-model analyses identified latent strategy signatures in LLM-based behavioural simulations and used them to guide refinements of classical decision-making models that improved held-out behavioural fit (Fig. 1g). Together, these results establish internal cognitive organization as an explanatory level connecting model mechanisms to capabilities and pathology, while providing common basis for comparison with human neural and behavioural data without implying biological equivalence.

sections.title: Results
sections.content: NeuroCogMap provides multi-level map of cognitive organization in LLMs Framework overview. To characterize how cognitive functions are internally organized in LLMs, we developed NeuroCogMap, multi-level framework inspired by cognitive neuroscience. NeuroCogMap links functional atlas of parcels with structural connectome among them, cognitive atlas assigning interpretable functions to those parcels, cognitive capabilities linked to weighted sets of parcels and cognitive hierarchy spanning perception, representation, abstraction and application (Fig. 1d). We constructed the framework by curating capability space and associated evaluation datasets, extracting sparse autoencoder (SAE) activations and clustering features according to their task-evoked response profiles [10, 23, 15]. We then estimated directed cross-layer structural connections among parcels, assigned parcel-level cognitive functions and linked parcels to capabilities using task-evoked activation, intervention effects and semantic consistency. Unless otherwise stated, analyses used models spanning parameter scales and training stages, including Gemma2-2B, Gemma2-9B-IT and Llama-3.1-8B [23, 24, 25]; implementation details are provided in Methods.

sections.title: Functional parcels emerge as stable and interpretable cognitive units in LLMs
sections.content: A central question for NeuroCogMap is whether internal LLM activity forms functionally meaningful units rather than arbitrary clusters of sparse features. We evaluated parcel organization along three criteria: stable granularity, internal coherence and cross-model consistency. Across candidate atlas granularities, joint score combining clustering quality, functional-description quality and functional non-redundancy peaked at 270 parcels (score = 0.771; Fig. 2a,a1; Supplementary Table 2), indicating an intermediatescale organization. Parcels were also internally coherent: semantic similarity among top-activating texts was consistently higher within than between parcels (mean intra-parcel similarity, 0.638; mean inter-parcel similarity, 0.433; 𝑊 = 36585.0, 𝑃 < 0.0001; Fig. 2a,a2; Supplementary Table 2). Finally, matched parcels showed substantially higher activation-pattern correspondence than baseline directions across Gemma2-2B, Gemma2-9B-IT and Llama-3.1-8B comparisons (Fig. 2a,a3), indicating that parcel-level organization is partially shared across models. Parcels were next evaluated for whether they encoded interpretable cognitive functions and whether such functions generalized across models. Functional descriptions generated from parcel activation profiles predicted parcel activation rankings on held-out examples. NeuroCogMap achieved higher prediction accuracy than random clustering, neuron-based and keyword-only baselines (two-sided paired Wilcoxon signed-rank tests with Holm correction, 𝑛 = 40; 𝑊+ = 673773, 𝑍 = 3.544.88, all 𝑃Holm 0.001; Fig. 2b,b1; Supplementary Table 2), indicating that atlas-derived descriptions captured predictive functional structure rather than plausible verbal summaries alone. Targeted intervention on parcel activations shifted model outputs in directions predicted by assigned parcel functions. Larger intervention success rates were associated with larger output changes (Fig. 2b,b2), showing that the targeted parcels made functionally consistent contributions to model outputs under perturbation. Parcel-level cognitive functions were also partially reproducible across models: matched parcels were substantially more likely than random baselines to exhibit identical or partially similar cognitive meanings across LLMs (Fig. 2b,b3). Together with the cross-model activation correspondence above, these results suggest that NeuroCogMap captures partially shared functional vocabulary across models at the level of both activation dynamics and cognitive interpretation. Parcels organize into cognitive capabilities and hierarchical layers. Having established parcels as stable and interpretable functional units, we next examined whether they formed selective links with higher-order cognitive capabilities. Parcels most strongly linked to given capability showed higher activation than unrelated parcels across top-ranked parcelcapability pairs (paired 𝑡-tests,𝑡45 = 2.75 4.02, all 𝑃 0.01; Fig. 2c,c1,left). The same selectivity was observed in the reciprocal analysis, where top-linked capabilities for fixed parcel were more strongly activated than unrelated capabilities (paired 𝑡-tests, 𝑡40 = 5.438.82, all 𝑃 < 0.0001; Fig. 2c,c1,right). We next tested whether these links were causally meaningful. Suppressing strongly linked parcels or capabilities produced significantly larger decreases in ground-truth log probability than suppressing unrelated units across both parcel-to-capability and reverse-direction perturbations (paired 𝑡-tests, 𝑡34 = 2.753.92 and 𝑡39 = 2.763.46, all 𝑃 < 0.01; Fig. 2c,c2; Supplementary Table 3). We next examined whether these capabilities formed meaningful hierarchy. Guided by Bloom-inspired levels of cognitive complexity [19], we organized cognitive capabilities into four operational layersperception, representation, abstraction and application (Defined in Methods and Supplementary Table 7) and tested whether this stratification was reflected in parcel structure. Parcels assigned to given layer were consistently more semantically similar to their own layer description than to descriptions of other layers (Fig. 2d,d1). This pattern was observed across perception, representation and abstraction layers (within-layer similarities, 0.541, 0.503 and 0.452, respectively; two-sided paired 𝑡-tests, 𝑡19 = 3.747.83, all 𝑃 < 0.001), while the application layer showed the same overall trend with significant separation from representation and abstraction layers (𝑃 = 0.017 and 𝑃 = 0.022; Fig. 2d,d1), supporting the semantic coherence of the proposed hierarchy. Critically, this hierarchy was also supported by evidence for operational prerequisite structure rather than descriptive grouping alone. Controlled synthetic benchmarks were designed to test whether lower-order retrieval and knowledge-recall support facilitated higher-order reasoning across the perception-to-abstraction (L1L3) and representation-to-abstraction (L2L3) contrasts while holding architecture and optimization fixed. To ensure fair comparison with matched training-example counts, Fig. 2d,d2 used Double L3 and Treble L3 as volume-matched L3-only baselines, constructed with twofold or threefold L3 training data to match the corresponding multi-level conditions. In the in-context regime, adding lower-level retrieval support

sections.content: improved two-hop reasoning relative to higher-level reasoning training alone (Welchs two-sample 𝑡-tests, 𝑛 = 5 runs per condition, 𝑡 = 5.9026.17, all 𝑃 < 0.001; Fig. 2d,d2). Adding lower-level knowledge-recall support similarly improved parametric reasoning (𝑡 = 6.679.08, all 𝑃 0.01; Fig. 2d,d2). In mixed reasoning tasks requiring both contextual and parametric routes, jointly providing both lower-level supports yielded the strongest performance across model scales (𝑡 = 14.6042.80, all 𝑃 < 0.001; Fig. 2d,d2). Additional semantic-dispersion and structural-degree analyses of hierarchy-associated parcels are reported in Extended Data Fig. 11, providing convergent support for the proposed hierarchy by showing that higher-level capabilities recruit more semantically diverse parcels, whereas lower-level parcels occupy more connected positions within the model-level structural connectome. Taken together, these results show that NeuroCogMap does not merely identify isolated functional parcels. Rather, it organizes them into structured cognitive system in which parcels map selectively and causally onto capabilities, and capabilities in turn form semantically coherent and functionally dependent hierarchy. This higher-order organization provides the basis for the pathology, brain-alignment and cognitive-model analyses that follow.

sections.title: NeuroCogMap reveals representational pathologies in LLMs
sections.content: Functional brain maps, lesion studies and pathology-based inference have long been used in neuroscience to relate behavioural symptoms to disruptions of distributed cognitive systems [16, 15, 17, 18, 20]. Motivated by this methodological logic, we asked whether an analogous cognitive map of LLMs could make model pathologies amenable to systematic analysis: revealing the internal systems associated with pathological generation, testing whether these signatures detect pathological outputs, and using them to guide targeted mitigation. To characterize the internal organization underlying pathological behaviour, we compared normative and pathological responses across four levels of NeuroCogMap: circuit connectivity, parcel activation, capability recruitment and hierarchical organization (Fig. 3a). Circuit connectivity refers to task-evoked coupling among functional parcels, estimated from answer-token trajectories and interpreted within the model-level structural connectome. This structural constraint is important because functional coupling alone does not establish directed influence. This contrastive framework allowed us to identify whether pathology emerged from disrupted coordination among structurally linked functional units, abnormal parcel recruitment, altered capability engagement or shifts across cognitive layers (See Methods). We first examined representational pathologies, in which factual or social representations are incorrectly selected, weighted or coordinated during generation. Here we focus on hallucination, with social-bias analyses providing parallel representational-related case in Extended Data Fig. 8. By contrast, behavioural-control pathologies, including refusal failure and sycophancy, primarily involve failures to regulate outputs under adversarial instruction or social pressure; this distinction was consistent with aggregate activation differences between representation-related and behavioural-control-related systems (Extended Data Fig. 7).

sections.title: Hallucination arises through distinct representational routes
sections.content: We first asked whether hallucination reflects single internal failure mode or multiple forms of representational pathology. To this end, we compared truthful and hallucinated responses on two complementary datasets: TruthfulQA, which contains questions designed to elicit common misconceptions [27], and NQ-Open, which consists of direct factual queries without misleading premises [28]. Dataset details and representative task patterns are summarized in Supplementary Table 1 and the Supplementary Information. NeuroCogMap revealed marked dissociation between these settings. Hallucinations in TruthfulQA primarily reflected failure of higher-order evaluative control over misleading representations, whereas hallucinations in NQ-Open reflected failure to coordinate otherwise relevant factual retrieval systems into coherent answer-grounding pathway (Fig. 3be). In TruthfulQA, hallucination reflected impaired evaluative control over misleading representations rather than absent factual retrieval. Truthful responses coupled retrieval to monitoring pathways, including VeracityCheck ModuleAffirmation Detector coupling (Δ𝑤 = 0.57, 𝑃 = 0.0087), whereas hallucinated responses strengthened local retrieval circuits such as Geographic RetrievalCountry Identification (Δ𝑤 = 0.68, 𝑃 = 0.026; Fig. 3b). Parcel activity showed the same shift: truthful responses preferentially recruited evaluative parcels, including Binary Fact-Check (Δ𝑎 = 47.82, 𝑃 = 0.0068) and Outcome Detection (Δ𝑎 = 21.10, 𝑃 = 0.038), whereas halluci-

sections.content: nated responses over-recruited encyclopaedic lookup parcels, including Country Identification (Δ𝑎 = 39.28, 𝑃 = 0.0052) and Celebrity Knowledge Retrieval (Δ𝑎 = 29.72, 𝑃 = 1.9 109; Fig. 3c). The capability profile and hierarchy showed the same pattern: truthful responses preferentially engaged Causal reasoning (Δ𝑎 = 15.50, 𝑃 = 2.0 105), Verification (Δ𝑎 = 11.80, 𝑃 = 2.7 104) and Judgment (Δ𝑎 = 9.96, 𝑃 = 4.5 105), together with higher-order abstraction and application layers, whereas hallucinated responses shifted towards retrieval-dominated generation and lower perception and representation layers (Fig. 3d,e). Thus, TruthfulQA hallucination was associated with locally reinforced associative retrieval that was insufficiently constrained by higher-order evaluative control. NQ-Open hallucinations followed distinct representational pattern, reflecting fragmentation of factualretrieval systems during direct factual answering. Truthful responses depended on coordinated factualaccess circuits, including stronger Internal Geopolitical-Trivia RetrievalReal-Name and Role Retrieval coupling (Δ𝑤 = 0.47, 𝑃 = 0.0017) and Geographic Knowledge RetrievalFact-Retrieval Core coupling (Δ𝑤 = 0.44, 𝑃 = 0.0051). Hallucinated responses retained retrieval activity but showed fragmented factual coordination, including weakened Internal Geopolitical-Trivia RetrievalWikipedia Fact Retrieval coupling (Δ𝑤 = 0.74, 𝑃 = 2.0 106; Fig. 3b). Parcel activity showed the same dissociation: truthful responses more strongly recruited Country Identification (Δ𝑎 = 39.66, 𝑃 = 2.0 105), Geographic Retrieval (Δ𝑎 = 22.55, 𝑃 = 2.9 104), Entity Recall (Δ𝑎 = 13.12, 𝑃 = 4.9 106) and Fact-Retrieval Core (Δ𝑎 = 11.86, 𝑃 = 1.1 105), whereas hallucinated responses disproportionately recruited Real-Name and Role Retrieval (Δ𝑎 = 22.11, 𝑃 = 1.4 1010; Fig. 3c). The capability profile and hierarchy further indicated disruption of distributed factual-answering regime. Truthful responses jointly engaged Context understanding (Δ𝑎 = 7.66, 𝑃 = 3.7 104), Knowledge learning (Δ𝑎 = 5.32, 𝑃 = 1.4 1021), Verification (Δ𝑎 = 3.63, 𝑃 = 2.0 108) and Information retrieval (Δ𝑎 = 3.28, 𝑃 = 0.0086), with broad engagement across all four hierarchy layers. Hallucinated responses instead disrupted this cross-layer coordination (Fig. 3d,e). Thus, NQ-Open hallucination reflected not selective loss of retrieval, but failure to coordinate factual access, contextual understanding, verification and answer generation into coherent response pathway. Together, these results show that hallucination is not mechanistically uniform. TruthfulQA hallucinations reflected insufficient higher-order monitoring of misleading premises and retrieved associations, whereas NQ-Open hallucinations reflected disrupted coordination among factual-retrieval modules. NeuroCogMap therefore characterizes hallucination not as generic generation error, but as systems-level disruption in how knowledge is retrieved, evaluated and coordinated during generation.

sections.title: Hallucination signatures support detection and targeted intervention
sections.content: We next asked whether the pathology signatures identified above were merely descriptive or could support prediction and intervention. We therefore constructed hallucination detector from NeuroCogMap-derived signatures spanning parcel recruitment, capability engagement and circuit organization. Responses were scored according to their alignment with truthful and hallucinatory pathology signatures (see Methods). Across both model sizes, NeuroCogMap outperformed uncertainty-based, self-consistency-based and representation-probing baselines (Fig. 3f; see Supplementary Information for baseline definitions). Across HaluEval [29], MedHallu [30], Dolly closed-QA [31], NQ-Open [28], SciQ [32] and TruthfulQA [27], NeuroCogMap achieved mean AUROCs of 0.681 in Gemma2-2B and 0.840 in Gemma2-9B-IT, exceeding SelfCheckGPT [33], hidden probing [34] and uncertainty-based methods [35, 36, 37]. These results indicate that hallucination was more accurately detected from structured pathology signatures than from token-level uncertainty or generic hidden-state separability. We then converted the same pathology map into an intervention target. Enhancing parcels under-recruited in truthful responses and suppressing parcels over-recruited in hallucinatory responses improved factual accuracy across MedHallu [30], NQ-Open [28] and TruthfulQA [27] in both model sizes, with the largest gains in settings characterized by impaired evaluative control or disrupted factual coordination (Fig. 3g). Additional analyses in Llama-3.1-8B further supported the cross-model generality of these pathology signatures for detection and intervention beyond the Gemma2 family (Extended Data Fig. 10).

NeuroCogMap reveals behavioural-control pathologies in LLMs We next examined behavioural-control pathologies, in which represented content is incorrectly inhibited, executed or socially modulated during generation. Here we focus on refusal failure, with sycophancy analyses providing parallel behavioural-control case in Extended Data Fig. 9. Consistently, both behaviours showed larger pathologynormal activation differences in behavioural-control-related parcels and capabilities than in representation-related systems (Extended Data Fig. 7). This suggests that behavioural-control pathologies arise from abnormal modulation of control systems rather than primary disruption of belief representations.

sections.title: Refusal failure reflects a shift from control to procedural execution
sections.content: We first examined refusal failure as prototypical behavioural-control pathology by comparing successful-refusal and refusal-failure responses on AdvBench [38]. NeuroCogMap revealed shift from monitoringevaluation negation control during successful refusal to planning and procedural execution during refusal failure, in which refusal-failure responses were associated with stronger activation of actionable response patterns (Fig. 4ad). Successful refusal engaged control circuits linking risk detection, negation and answer gating, with representative increases in Negative-Motivation DetectorNegation and Self-Reference coupling (Δ𝑤 = 1.21, 𝑃 < 10300), whereas refusal failure strengthened execution-oriented pathways such as Idea and Plan DetectionProcedural Guidance coupling (Δ𝑤 = 0.36, 𝑃 = 4.5 1038; Fig. 4a). Parcel activity showed the same transition: successful refusal preferentially recruited Legal-Moral Evaluation and the Privacy and Refusal Engine, including large increase in Legal-Moral Evaluation (Δ𝑎 = 105.48), whereas refusal failure activated Illicit Behaviour Processing and Concrete Action Processing (Fig. 4b). At the capability level, successful refusal engaged Ethical reasoning, Alignment and Safety, whereas refusal failure shifted towards adaptive generation capabilities, including Zero-shot learning and Controllable generation (Fig. 4c). This dissociation was concentrated in the Situated Application and Social Interaction layer, indicating that refusal failure arose primarily from shift in behavioural control rather than from failure to parse or represent harmful content (Fig. 4d). Together,

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: these results characterize refusal failure as behavioural-control pathology in which harmful instructions are rerouted from safety-related response selection towards procedural action generation. Refusal-failure signatures support detection and targeted intervention We next asked whether the refusal-failure signatures identified above could support prediction and intervention. Using NeuroCogMap-derived signatures spanning circuit, parcel and capability levels, we constructed detector to distinguish successful-refusal from refusal-failure responses and used the same pathology map to guide parcel-level intervention (Fig. 4e,f). Across AdvBench [38] and JBB-Behaviors [39], NeuroCogMap achieved near-ceiling refusal-failure detection in both Gemma2-2B and Gemma2-9B-IT (mean AUROC, 0.992 and 0.990), outperforming perplexity-, logitsand perturbation-based baselines [40, 41, 42] (Fig. 4e; see Supplementary Information for baseline definitions). Thus, refusal failure was better detected as structured breakdown in behavioural-control organization than as generic change in token surprise, output logits or perturbation sensitivity. NeuroCogMap-guided steering substantially improved refusal accuracy when residual control pathology remained, including large gain on AdvBench [38] in Gemma2-2B (38.3% to 98.6%), while effects were constrained when baseline refusal was already near ceiling (Fig. 4f). Additional analyses in Llama-3.1-8B [25] further supported the cross-model generality of these pathology signatures for detection and intervention beyond the Gemma2 family (Extended Data Fig. 10).

sections.title: NeuroCogMap reveals convergent functional organization in LLMs and human cortex
sections.content: Having shown that NeuroCogMap-derived organization characterizes and modulates LLM failure modes, we next asked whether the same functional organization also relates to external neural measurements. Recent studies have shown that LLM activations can predict human brain responses during naturalistic language comprehension, suggesting that LLMs may provide computationally explicit models of cortical language processing [43, 44, 45, 46]. However, prediction from generic hidden states does not establish whether this correspondence reflects an organized functional structure. We therefore asked whether the organization revealed by NeuroCogMap corresponds to human cortical function at the levels of parcel activity, cortical systems and representational geometry. Specifically, we tested whether NeuroCogMap parcels predict cortical responses better and whether the resulting parcelcortex alignments preserve cognitive-functional and representational structure within cortical systems. NeuroCogMap predicts cortical responses in higher-order networks We first evaluated this correspondence using encoding models of naturalistic language comprehension. Using the LeBel story-listening fMRI dataset, in which participants listened to extended autobiographical narratives [47], we mapped cortical blood-oxygen-level-dependent (BOLD) responses, an indirect haemodynamic measure of neural activity, onto the 100-parcel Schaefer cortical atlas and grouped the resulting parcel-level responses according to the Yeo seven-network organization for system-level analyses [15, 14]. NeuroCogMap parcel activations of LLMs were then entered jointly into ridge-regression encoding models to predict held-out cortical BOLD responses (See Methods). NeuroCogMap achieved the highest prediction accuracy among all compared representations (mean 𝑟 = 0.407; Fig. 5a, left). It outperformed lexical and embedding-based baselines, including WordRate [17] (𝑟 = 0.123), Word2Vec [48] (𝑟 = 0.158) and BERT [44, 49] (𝑟 = 0.219), as well as LLM-feature baselines, including Language Context [45] (𝑟 = 0.361) and Language Standard [50, 46] (𝑟 = 0.367). All comparisons against NeuroCogMap were significant after BenjaminiHochberg correction [51]. Thus, structured parcel-level activity captured cortical variance beyond conventional embeddings and generic LLM features. The spatial distribution of this improvement localized the NeuroCogMap advantage to higher-order association cortex (Fig. 5a, right). Relative to Language Standard, the largest gains were concentrated in language-relevant Default network regions, including left parietal language-comprehension (Δ𝑟 = 0.268), left prefrontal semantic-retrieval (Δ𝑟 = 0.174) and left temporal language parcels (Δ𝑟 = 0.174), with additional gains in right temporal Default regions (Δ𝑟 = 0.190 and Δ𝑟 = 0.113). This localization suggests sensitivity to the semantic retrieval and contextual integration demands of narrative comprehension.

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: Figure 5 NeuroCogMap reveals convergent functional organization in LLMs and human cortex. a, NeuroCogMap outperforms language-feature baselines in predicting human cortical responses in the LeBel story-listening fMRI dataset. Bars show Fisher-z-transformed mean parcel-wise correlations, back-transformed to Pearsons 𝑟, across 10 language-related cortical parcels; error bars indicate 95% confidence intervals across cortical parcels. Labels indicate BenjaminiHochberg-adjusted paired 𝑡-tests against NeuroCogMap. Cortical maps show parcel-wise prediction accuracy for Language (Standard) and NeuroCogMap. b, Prediction accuracy across Yeo 7 cortical networks. Bars show Fisher-z-transformed mean parcel-wise correlations, back-transformed to Pearsons 𝑟; dots denote cortical parcels and error bars indicate 95% confidence intervals across cortical parcels within each network. c, Cognitive-functional correspondence between human cortical parcels and NeuroCogMap parcels. Top, semantic similarity between Neurosynth-derived human parcel profiles and functional descriptions of the top-10 or bottom-10 LLM parcels ranked by neural prediction accuracy, computed using Qwen3-Embedding-8B cosine similarity. Bottom, LLM-judge evaluation of functional correspondence; scores range from 0 to 1. d, Case studies of humanLLM cognitive correspondences in the held-out story Where Theres Smoke. Representative matches are shown for dorsal-attention, default-mode, control-monitoring and social-affective systems. Rank denotes the matched LLM parcels neural-prediction rank for the corresponding human parcel; Sim. denotes semantic similarity. e, Description-based network-level representational similarity analysis (RSA). Upper heat maps show RSA among human cortical parcels computed from Neurosynth cognitive profiles; lower heat maps show RSA among aligned NeuroCogMap parcels computed from matched LLM parcel descriptions. Pearson correlations between vectorized upper-triangular matrices quantify humanLLM representational alignment. Vis, Visual; SomMot, Somatomotor; DorsAttn, Dorsal Attention; SalVentAttn, Salience/Ventral Attention; Limbic, Limbic; Cont, Frontoparietal Control; Default, Default Mode.

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: Aggregating prediction accuracy within Yeo 7 functional networks confirmed this systems-level localization (Fig. 5b). Accuracy was highest in Default, Frontoparietal Control and Salience/Ventral Attention networks, and lower in primary sensory systems, consistent with the semantic integration and control demands of naturalistic story comprehension.

sections.title: NeuroCogMap captures semantic and representational correspondence with human cortical function
sections.content: We next tested whether neural predictability was accompanied by cognitive-functional correspondence. Human cortical parcels were assigned Neurosynth-derived functional profiles across Cognitive Atlas terms [52], and NeuroCogMap parcels were ranked for each cortical parcel by prediction accuracy. We then compared the functional descriptions of topand bottom-ranked LLM parcels with the corresponding cortical profile using text-embedding cosine similarity [53]. Across most cortical systems, top-ranked NeuroCogMap parcels showed higher semantic similarity to human parcel functions than bottom-ranked parcels (Fig. 5c). This separation was strongest in higher-order systems, including Dorsal Attention, Salience/Ventral Attention, Frontoparietal Control and Default networks, and weaker in Somatomotor and Limbic systems; LLM-judge evaluation showed the same pattern. Thus, neural predictability was systematically coupled with cognitive-functional agreement: the LLM parcels that best predicted cortical parcel also tended to express functions closer to its meta-analytic cognitive profile. This correspondence was also evident in individual narratives. In the held-out story Where Theres Smoke, representative humanLLM matches formed an interpretable systems-level pattern spanning attention, memory, control and social-affective processing (Fig. 5d). For example, episodic memory retrieval in the default-mode system aligned with an entity-recall module (rank = 1, PCC = 0.455, similarity = 0.640). Thus, humanLLM correspondence extended beyond isolated parcel matches to coordinated functional systems recruited during naturalistic narrative understanding. Finally, we tested whether this correspondence extended from parcel-wise matching to within-network representational geometry using description-based Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) [54]. In Salience/Ventral Attention, Frontoparietal Control and Default networks, we compared Neurosynth-derived human parcel similarity matrices with NeuroCogMap similarity matrices constructed from the matched LLM parcel descriptions. This analysis revealed structured but selective hemispheric pattern (Fig. 5e): alignment was significant in the left Default network (𝑟 = 0.225, 𝑃 = 0.047) and strongest in the right Frontoparietal Control network (𝑟 = 0.774, 𝑃 = 3.1 108), with positive trend in right Salience/Ventral Attention (𝑟 = 0.473, 𝑃 = 0.054) and weaker effects elsewhere. Thus, aligned NeuroCogMap parcels showed both functional consistency and partial preservation of within-network representational structure in higher-order cortical systems. Together, these analyses reveal multilevel correspondence between NeuroCogMap and the human cortex. NeuroCogMap parcel activity predicted cortical responses, and the best-aligned parcels showed both functional consistency and partial preservation of within-network representational geometry. NeuroCogMap therefore provides structured basis for relating LLM internal organization to the functional architecture of the human cortex.

sections.title: NeuroCogMap enables cognitively grounded model discovery from LLMs
sections.content: Model-based fMRI and computational cognitive neuroscience show that neural constraints can help distinguish latent decision mechanisms that behaviour alone may underdetermine, including model-based inference, modelfree prediction errors and strategy arbitration [59, 60, 61, 55]. Having established measurable correspondence between NeuroCogMap parcel activity and human cortical responses, we asked whether internal signatures observed during LLM-based behavioural simulation could support the discovery of explicit cognitive mechanisms. We first established that these signatures captured decision-relevant variation in human neural activity and in the ability of LLMs to reproduce individual behaviour (Fig. 6ac). On this basis, we then translated behaviour-linked internal signatures into candidate mechanisms for refining classical cognitive model (Fig. 6df).

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: Figure 6 NeuroCogMap enables cognitively grounded model discovery from LLMs. a, Prediction of human neural activity during the two-step decision-making task using fMRI data from [55]. Bars show mean participant-wise prediction accuracy across 10 conditionstratified participants; error bars denote 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. BenjaminiHochberg-adjusted 𝑞 values indicate Fisher-𝑧-based comparisons between each baseline and NeuroCogMap. b, Prediction of behavioural fit across five cognitive experiments. Bars show Pearson correlations between NeuroCogMap-derived representations and LLM behavioural fit across subjects. Significance was assessed by two-sided tests of Pearson correlation against 𝜌 = 0; 𝑃 < 0.05, 𝑃 < 0.01, 𝑃 < 0.001. c, Parcel-level interpretation of behavioural-fit prediction. For each task family, parcels are ranked by their parcel-wise correlation with AIC-based behavioural fit and grouped into beneficial and interfering contributors. Parcel descriptions summarize the cognitive processes associated with stronger or weaker fit. Beneficial and interfering contributors correspond to negative and positive parcel-wise correlations with AIC, respectively; lower AIC indicates better behavioural fit. d, Held-out evaluation on two independent two-step task datasets. Bars show AIC values for the classical cognitive model, the behaviour-discovered model and the NeuroCogMap-discovered model; lower AIC indicates better fit. Two Step Task One used data from [56] (51,500 trials, 206 participants), and Two Step Task Two used data from [57] (40,000 trials, 100 participants). Dashed lines indicate random-guessing baselines following [58]. e, NeuroCogMap-guided cognitive-model discovery pipeline. Behavioural data, baseline model fits and NeuroCogMap latent activations are analysed by reasoning agent to propose candidate strategies, which are converted by model-writing agent into executable cognitive-model code. f, Schematic summary of NeuroCogMap-guided refinement of the Dual-systems Model, linking behavioural evidence and NeuroCogMap-derived interpretability signals to model extensions and improved behavioural fit. NeuroCogMap predicts neural responses during two-step decision-making We first asked whether NeuroCogMap captured neural variation during the same decision paradigm used for subsequent model discovery. Using two-step-task fMRI data [55], we predicted participant-wise brain activity from NeuroCogMap parcel activations with the same encoding framework used in Fig. 5. For computational efficiency, we analysed condition-stratified subset of 10 participants, each contributing approximately 300 choices from either the magic-carpet or abstract task version (See Methods). NeuroCogMap achieved the highest

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: prediction accuracy among all compared representations (mean 𝑟 = 0.355; Fig. 6a), outperforming Word2Vec (𝑟 = 0.205), BERT (𝑟 = 0.339), Language (Context) (𝑟 = 0.344) and Language (Standard) (𝑟 = 0.341). Thus, NeuroCogMap parcel activations contained information predictive of human neural responses during two-step decision-making, providing an external neural test of their relevance to the target cognitive domain.

sections.title: NeuroCogMap predicts behavioural fit across cognitive tasks
sections.content: We next asked whether NeuroCogMap representations captured variation in how closely the LLM behavioural simulator reproduced individual human behavioural responses across five diverse cognitive experiments. These experiments were drawn from the Psych-101 dataset [58] and spanned episodic long-term memory, multi-attribute decision-making, Shepard categorization, intertemporal choice and drifting four-armed bandit learning [62, 63, 64, 65, 66]. We quantified behavioural fit using the Akaike information criterion (AIC) [67], with lower AIC indicating closer correspondence to human behaviour, and tested whether NeuroCogMap parcel activations predicted this fit across subjects. Across all five task families, NeuroCogMap-derived representations significantly predicted behavioural fit (Fig. 6b). Prediction was strongest for episodic long-term memory (𝑟 = 0.598), followed by multi-attribute decision-making (𝑟 = 0.556), Shepard categorization (𝑟 = 0.522) and intertemporal choice (𝑟 = 0.508). weaker but significant association was also observed for the drifting four-armed bandit task (𝑟 = 0.213). Thus, NeuroCogMap parcel activity predicted which LLMs better approximated human behaviour, complementing the neural correspondence observed above. Parcel-level analyses revealed the internal configurations underlying these behavioural-fit predictions (Fig. 6c). Parcels associated with better fit supported task-appropriate computations, including uncertainty tracking in the drifting bandit task and verification/entity retrieval in episodic memory, whereas poorer fit reflected task-misaligned or overly local processes such as shortcut retrieval, premature answer closure or irrelevant perceptual-detail extraction. Thus, NeuroCogMap did not reveal single global signature of humanlike behaviour, but resolved task-specific internal configurations that made an LLM better or worse behavioural model.

sections.title: NeuroCogMap guides refinement of classical cognitive models
sections.content: Having linked NeuroCogMap to neural activity and behavioural fit, we asked whether the same latent structure could guide explicit refinement of cognitive models. In the two-step task, standard paradigm for dissociating competing decision strategies, we compared classical Dual-systems Model with LLM-based behavioural simulators. In the training set, we identified participants for whom the LLM fit human choices better than the classical model, as indexed by lower AIC, and used NeuroCogMap to identify the parceland capability-level strategies associated with this behavioural advantage (Fig. 6e, see Methods). The baseline Dual-systems Model assumes arbitration between model-based and model-free valuation, but does not explicitly capture omission, uncertainty-dependent control or higher-order policy switching. Behavioural traces alone suggested richer decision dynamics in LLM-favoured participants, whereas NeuroCogMap linked this advantage to latent processes including outcome tracking, uncertainty-aware arbitration and policy switching (Fig. 6f). Guided by these signals, we extended the classical model with interpretable components targeting omission, dynamic arbitration, history dependence and rule-like switching. The resulting NeuroCogMap-discovered model yielded better held-out fits than both the original cognitive model and comparison model discovered from behavioural traces alone. On Two Step Task One [56], it achieved the lowest AIC (262.45), outperforming both the original model (268.77) and the behaviouronly discovered model (268.73). On Two Step Task Two [57], it again achieved the lowest AIC (465.95), improving over the original model (478.29) and behaviour-only discovered model (473.53; Fig. 6d). The behaviour-only comparison used the same agents, search budget and screening protocol, and AIC penalized implementation-level parameter counts. The lower held-out AIC therefore indicates that parceland capabilitylevel NeuroCogMap summaries provided additional information beyond behavioural traces alone. Together, these findings show that NeuroCogMap can do more than interpret LLM internal states. By exposing latent computational strategies that are not directly transparent from behaviour alone, it provides route for translating LLM internal organization into explicit, testable refinements of cognitive models.

sections.title: Discussion
sections.content: NeuroCogMap provides system-level map of cognitive organization in large language models. By linking internal features to functional parcels, cognitive descriptions, capability mappings and cognitive hierarchy, it defines mesoscopic scale that is coarse enough to reveal regularities across models, yet specific enough to support prediction, perturbation and behavioural interpretation. This reframes interpretability as cognitiveneuroscience-style analysis of artificial systems, connecting internal computation to behaviour and pathology and relating artificial organization to human cortical function and cognitive modelling [10, 12, 13]. The pathology analyses show why this organizational layer is explanatory rather than merely descriptive. Hallucination and social bias were associated with disruptions in representational selection, evaluation and coordination, whereas refusal failure and sycophancy reflected failures in regulating whether represented content was expressed, inhibited or independently evaluated [68, 37, 8, 69, 70, 71]. These findings suggest that model failures are not uniformly reducible to uncertainty, surface errors or generic hidden-state separability, but can arise from separable breakdowns in the recruitment and coordination of internal cognitive systems. The same multilevel signatures that distinguished these failures also supported detection and mechanism-guided steering. NeuroCogMap therefore does not claim to solve alignment or safety; rather, it shows how diagnosis and intervention can be grounded in internal cognitive organization instead of relying only on output-level labels or post hoc filtering. Beyond its role in analysing model pathology, NeuroCogMap provides way to relate artificial and biological cognition without assuming mechanistic equivalence between language models and brains. The humanalignment analyses showed that NeuroCogMap-derived representations predicted cortical responses and exhibited functional correspondence with higher-order cortical systems associated with semantic integration, control and context-dependent interpretation. The model-discovery analyses further showed that internal model organization can reveal candidate latent strategies that, when incorporated into explicit cognitive models, improve accounts of human decision-making. LLMs are therefore treated not as replicas of human cognition, but as manipulable artificial systems whose internal structure can be used to generate hypotheses about cognitive organization and test them against neural and behavioural data [4, 43, 54, 61]. This positions LLMs as experimental systems for testing principles of organized function across artificial and biological intelligence. NeuroCogMap bridges brain-inspired artificial intelligence and mechanistic interpretability by using cognitive neuroscience as methodological template rather than only as source of architectural analogies [1, 72, 9, 10, 12, 13]. Task-evoked mapping, functional parcellation, cognitive atlases, representational analysis, pathology-based inference and causal intervention together enable LLMs to be studied as organized cognitive systems, rather than treated either as behavioural black boxes or as collections of isolated local mechanisms [16, 73, 74, 54, 20]. Using cognitive neuroscience as methodological template nevertheless requires clear distinction between biological and artificial parcels. Cortical parcellations divide spatially continuous tissue into localized regions, whereas NeuroCogMap parcels are non-spatial sets of latent features grouped primarily by functional response similarity, with layer structure used only as regularizing constraint, and may therefore span transformer layers [16, 15]. Their correspondence with the brain is tested at the level of functional organization, not spatial or mechanistic equivalence, consistent with the distributed and overlapping organization of human cognition [18, 17]. The same qualification applies to cognitive capabilities: shared task labels do not imply computational equivalence across systems. Information requiring memory encoding and later recall in humans, for example, may remain directly accessible in an LLM context window and therefore require contextual retrieval rather than memory [75]. NeuroCogMap consequently defines capabilities from LLM-specific tasks as task-grounded, distributed functional constructs rather than localized modules or direct counterparts of human cognitive faculties. Several boundaries qualify these conclusions. NeuroCogMap was evaluated on representative Gemma and LLaMA models spanning pretrained and instruction-tuned stages [24, 25], and the cross-model analyses indicate that the recovered organization is not specific to single checkpoint or training regime. Extending the framework to larger models, additional architectures and broader post-training procedures will be important for separating stable organizational principles from architecture-, scaleand task-specific contingencies. related limitation is that the present analyses focus on language models rather than multimodal systems. This choice reflects the current maturity of text-only transformer architectures, where shared design principles and large-scale training regimes have produced relatively stable internal organization. By contrast, multimodal models still vary substantially in how visual, auditory or other sensory streams are encoded, aligned and

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: integrated with language [76, 77, 78]. Applying NeuroCogMap to such systems will therefore be an important next step, especially for testing whether functional parcels extend across modalities or whether multimodal cognition requires distinct forms of cross-modal organization. Future work could extend NeuroCogMap from static maps toward dynamic and causal models of internal state trajectories, directed interactions and systematic perturbations [79, 80, 81]. Such progress would move the study of LLMs toward cognitive neuroscience of artificial intelligence, in which cognitive organization becomes an experimentally tractable object across artificial and biological systems.

sections.title: Construction of NeuroCogMap
sections.content: NeuroCogMap maps internal cognitive organization in large language models (LLMs) through four linked representations: functional parcels, parcel-level cognitive descriptions, capability-level mappings and hierarchy of cognitive operations. The framework was constructed in four stages. First, we curated capability space and associated evaluation datasets from recent LLM literature. Second, we extracted sparse autoencoder (SAE) activations from LLMs over these datasets and clustered SAE features according to their response profiles to obtain parcel-like functional units. Third, we assigned each parcel an interpretable cognitive description from its high-activation examples and linked parcels to curated capability inventory using activation, intervention and semantic evidence. Fourth, we organized capabilities into hierarchy spanning perception, representation, abstraction and application.

sections.title: Literature corpus and capability curation
sections.content: We assembled corpus of publicly available conference-paper records and metadata for major AI conferences between 2023 and 2025, including ACL, ICLR, NeurIPS and ICML, using sources such as OpenReview [82] when available. Records were retained when their metadata or text contained large language model or closely related variants, yielding 5,683 LLM-related papers. From this corpus, we extracted candidate capability terms, benchmark datasets and task descriptions. Candidate terms were retained when they denoted model abilities, cognitive operations, evaluation targets or task families, rather than model names or implementation details alone. Semantically overlapping terms were merged into normalized capability inventory, and each capability was linked to benchmark datasets that operationalized the corresponding ability in the source literature. The final capability space contained 45 cognitive capabilities. Capability definitions and dataset mappings are reported in Supplementary Tables 8 and 9.

sections.title: Models and sparse feature extraction
sections.content: Unless otherwise stated, analyses used Gemma2-2B, Gemma2-9B-IT and Llama-3.1-8B [23, 24, 25]. Gemma22B and Llama-3.1-8B were treated as pretrained base models, and Gemma2-9B-IT as an instruction-tuned model. Examples were represented as questionanswer pairs. For base models, the question and target answer were concatenated directly; for the instruction-tuned model, the same content was converted to the model-specific chat template. Inputs were truncated to 1,024 tokens and inference was run in bfloat16 precision. We represented internal activity in SAE feature space rather than raw neuron space, because individual model neurons are often polysemantic and can respond to multiple unrelated features, making neuron-level activations difficult to assign to stable functional roles [10]. SAEs provide sparse, overcomplete latent basis that aims to decompose such polysemantic activations into more separable and interpretable feature directions. For residual-stream vector hℓ,𝑡 ℝ𝑑model at layer ℓ and token position 𝑡, the SAE for that layer encoded sparse latent activations and reconstructed the residual stream as aℓ,𝑡 = 𝜙ℓ (cid:16) 𝑊enc ℓ (hℓ,𝑡 𝑏dec ℓ ) + 𝑏enc ℓ (cid:17) , (cid:98)hℓ,𝑡 = 𝑏dec ℓ + 𝑓 𝐹ℓ 𝑎ℓ,𝑡, 𝑓 𝑊dec ℓ 𝑓 , where 𝜙ℓ is the sparse activation function used by the released SAE checkpoint, 𝐹ℓ is the set of latents at layer ℓ, 𝑎ℓ,𝑡, 𝑓 is the activation of latent 𝑓 , and 𝑊enc ℓ 𝑓 and 𝑊dec ℓ 𝑓 denote its encoder and decoder directions. This representation is therefore better suited for functional parcellation, cross-model feature comparison and

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: targeted intervention through decoder directions. Consistent with this choice, the function-predictability validation in Fig. 2b1 included neuron-based baseline, which performed worse than the SAE-feature-derived parcel representation on held-out datasets. Gemma2-2B used Gemma Scope residual-stream SAEs for layers 025, Gemma2-9B-IT used Gemma Scope residual-stream SAEs for layers 9, 20 and 31 [23], and Llama-3.1-8B used Llama Scope LXR-8x SAEs for layers 031 [83]. Gemma SAEs contained 16,384 latents per layer, and Llama SAEs contained 32,768 latents per layer. Activations were extracted over the answer span. For each example, non-special tokens overlapping the answer were identified from tokenizer offsets, split into sentence-level units and averaged within each sentence. Sentence-level SAE activation vectors were stored as sparse matrices together with question, answer, sentenceboundary, token-boundary and dataset metadata. The functional atlas used sentence-level samples as its primary observations; datasetand example-level summaries were obtained by token-count-weighted averaging.

sections.title: Functional atlas construction
sections.content: For each model, sample-by-feature activation matrix 𝐴 was assembled from sentence-level SAE activations. Rows corresponded to retained sentence samples and columns corresponded to concatenated SAE features across selected layers. To ensure that the atlas reflected informative and task-responsive SAE latents rather than inactive, near-constant or broadly unselective features, we screened features before clustering. Features were retained only if their non-zero activation rate exceeded model-specific threshold and their variance exceeded 106. The non-zero activation threshold was 0.03 for Gemma models and 0.01 for Llama-3.1-8B. Samples whose total activation fell in the bottom 10% were removed. For each feature activation profile 𝑥, we computed 𝐺(𝑥) = 1 mean(𝑥)2 mean(𝑥2) . This quantity is selectivity index for the activation profile: it is low when feature responds broadly across samples and high when activation is concentrated in small subset of samples. Features below the specified selectivity threshold were removed; the retained quantile was 0.5 for Gemma2-2B and 0.8 for Gemma2-9B-IT and Llama-3.1-8B. The remaining feature profiles were L2-normalized and reduced by truncated singular value decomposition, with the number of components chosen as the smallest value explaining at least 80% of the variance.

sections.title: SAE features were clustered by minimizing an assignment cost that combined functional similarity and
sections.content: layer-distribution regularization. For feature 𝑗 in layer ℓ𝑗 and candidate parcel 𝑝, the assignment cost was 𝐽 ( 𝑗, 𝑝) = 1 cos(𝑧 𝑗, 𝜇 𝑝) + 𝜆layer 𝑟𝑝,ℓ ℓ ℓ𝑗, ℓ where 𝑧 𝑗 is the normalized embedding of feature 𝑗, 𝜇 𝑝 is the normalized mean embedding of parcel 𝑝, and 𝑟𝑝,ℓ is the current fraction of features in parcel 𝑝 assigned to layer ℓ. The second term penalizes assigning feature to parcels whose current layer distribution is far from the features layer, encouraging layer-coherent but not layer-exclusive parcels. We used 𝜆layer = 0.01. Empty parcels were reinitialized with real feature vectors, and computations were chunked over features to reduce memory use. For the primary Gemma2-2B atlas, candidate parcel numbers 𝑘 = 10, 20, . . . , 300 were evaluated. Clustering quality was quantified using the gap statistic [84], functional-description quality was estimated from LLMassisted expert scoring of parcel descriptions, and non-redundancy was estimated from semantic similarity and LLM confirmation of redundant parcel descriptions. The integrated score was 𝐶(𝑘) = 0.4 (cid:103)Gap(𝑘) + 0.3 (cid:101)𝑄(𝑘) + 0.3 [1 (cid:101)𝑅(𝑘)], where 𝑄(𝑘) denotes functional-description quality, 𝑅(𝑘) denotes redundancy and tildes denote minmax normalization across candidate 𝑘 values. The maximum occurred at 𝑘 = 270. Hyperparameter stability was evaluated across 16 settings obtained by varying SVD variance retention and clustering iterations; results are reported in Supplementary Table 4. Latent-to-parcel assignment summaries are reported in Supplementary Table 12. For this score, 𝑄(𝑘) was computed from structured LLM ratings of the evaluated parcel descriptions, using the same evidence provided during parcel annotation: activation-weighted keywords, dataset-distribution summaries and representative high-activation examples. The rubric asked whether each description was specific,

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: faithful to the high-activation examples and covered the dominant activation pattern, with ratings rescaled to common 01 range before averaging across evaluated parcels. Redundancy 𝑅(𝑘) was estimated by screening parcel-description pairs with semantic similarity and then using the same structured LLM judgement procedure to confirm whether pairs described identical or highly overlapping functions. Thus, 𝐶(𝑘) combined one unsupervised clustering criterion with two interpretation-oriented criteria, and the LLM-assisted components were used only to choose stable descriptive granularity rather than as independent validation of the atlas. targeted blinded human audit of sampled parcel descriptions is reported in Supplementary Table 6.

sections.title: Structural connectome
sections.content: In brain-network analysis, structural connectivity matrices summarize estimated connection strengths among parcellated regions and provide substrate for modelling system-level organization [85]. We constructed an analogous model-level structural connectome by estimating directed relationships among NeuroCogMap parcels. Because each parcel contains sparse features distributed across model layers, parcel-level connections were computed by first estimating forward cross-layer relationships between the features assigned to each parcel pair and then aggregating these relationships at the parcel level. For source SAE latent 𝑓 in layer ℓ𝑓 and target SAE latent 𝑔 in layer ℓ𝑔, only forward cross-layer pairs satisfying ℓ𝑓 < ℓ𝑔 were used. The feature-level edge was 𝑠( 𝑓 𝑔) = (cid:42) 𝑊dec ℓ𝑓 𝑓 ℓ𝑓 𝑓 2 + 𝜖 𝑊dec , 𝑊enc ℓ𝑔 𝑔 ℓ𝑔 𝑔 2 + 𝜖 𝑊enc (cid:43) , with 𝜖 = 1012. For source parcel 𝑝 and target parcel 𝑞, the directed structural edge was 𝑆𝑝𝑞 = 1 𝑝,𝑞 ( 𝑓 ,𝑔) 𝑝,𝑞 𝑠( 𝑓 𝑔), 𝑝,𝑞 = {( 𝑓 , 𝑔) : 𝑓 𝐹 𝑝, 𝑔 𝐹𝑞, ℓ𝑓 < ℓ𝑔}. This formula was applied when 𝑝,𝑞 > 0. If no valid forward cross-layer feature pairs existed for parcel pair, the connection strength was set to zero. The resulting matrix was used to constrain interpretation of functional connectivity during pathology analyses.

sections.title: Cognitive atlas construction
sections.content: The cognitive atlas assigns each functional parcel natural-language description of its dominant computation. For each parcel, up to 1,000 high-activation samples were retained for keyword extraction and datasetdistribution estimation, and the top 50 examples were displayed to the annotating model. If question exceeded 200 tokens, it was summarized before insertion into the prompt; if summarization failed, it was truncated to 200 tokens. Keywords were extracted from the concatenated question, answer and activated sentence using activation-weighted TFIDF with unigrams and bigrams. The top 20 keywords were retained, and the top 15 were included in the annotation prompt. The annotation input contained activation-weighted keywords, dataset-distribution summaries and representative high-activation samples. The annotating model returned concise function name, 100200 word function description and short account of the parcels role in model behaviour. Unless otherwise specified, LLM-assisted annotation, redundancy review, parcel ranking, cross-model parcel judgement, pathology labelling and humanLLM functional correspondence judgement used GPT-OSS-20B [86] served through local inference endpoint, with structured outputs required where downstream parsing was needed. GPT-5.2 [87] was used only for the model-discovery agents described below. The resulting Gemma2-2B parcel atlas is reported in Supplementary Table 10. Prompt templates, input fields and output schemas are provided in the Supplementary prompt templates section. We performed targeted blinded human audit of judgement-dependent components rather than full manual relabelling. Three annotators independently evaluated sampled annotation files, including 60 Gemma2-2B parcel descriptions, 570 pathology-labelled responses and 100 cross-model matched parcel pairs. Parcel descriptions were rated for specificity, faithfulness to high-activation examples, coverage of the dominant

sections.content: activation pattern and overreach; pathology labels and cross-model matches were evaluated with task-specific rubrics. Categorical disagreements were summarized by majority vote, ordinal ratings by the mean across annotators, and no-majority or ambiguous cases were retained as such or excluded from coarse agreement calculations where specified. Inter-annotator agreement and audit outcomes are reported in Supplementary Table 6. Thus, the LLM-assisted components supported semantic annotation and organization, whereas key validation endpoints were empirical activation rankings, cross-model activation correspondence, perturbation effects and blinded human audit. Parcelcapability mapping and hierarchy Adapting the logic of functional parcellation in cognitive neuroscience to model activations, NeuroCogMap defines parcels as non-spatial, mesoscopic functional units in SAE-feature space: groups of sparse features with similar task-evoked response profiles and coherent functional annotations. Capabilities summarize higherorder task-evoked functional dimensions supported by weighted parcel sets, and the hierarchy provides coarse operational ordering from perceptual access and representation to abstract reasoning and situated application. Parcelcapability links combined task-evoked activation, intervention evidence and semantic consistency. Candidate capabilitydataset links were inherited from the same source papers used to define the capability inventory: when paper described capability together with benchmark tasks or evaluation datasets, those datasets were treated as operationalizations of that capability. These automatically extracted links were manually reviewed to remove model names, implementation details and weak or ambiguous datasetcapability associations. The top 50 activation-ranked parcels per dataset were used as candidates for intervention. For parcel 𝑝, steering added the SAE decoder directions of its latents at each corresponding residual-stream hook point, ℓ,𝑡 = ℎℓ,𝑡 + 𝛾 ℎ 𝑚 𝑓 𝑊dec ℓ 𝑓 , 𝑓 𝐹 𝑝,ℓ where 𝐹 𝑝,ℓ is the subset of parcel 𝑝 at layer ℓ, 𝑡 indexes the token position and 𝑚 𝑓 is the maximum observed activation of feature 𝑓 in the evaluation corpus. Positive steering used 𝛾 = 0.5 and suppressive steering used 𝛾 = 1.0. Interventions used up to 128 examples per dataset unless otherwise specified. Model performance was quantified as the mean conditional log probability of the ground-truth answer tokens over the answer span. Let ℓ0 𝑑 ( 𝑝) denote the baseline, positive-steering and suppressive-steering mean log probabilities for dataset 𝑑 and parcel 𝑝. We defined 𝑑 ( 𝑝) and ℓ 𝑑 , ℓ+ 𝑑 ( 𝑝) = max{0, ℓ+ Δ+ 𝑑 ( 𝑝) ℓ0 𝑑 }, Δ 𝑑 ( 𝑝) = max{0, ℓ0 𝑑 ℓ 𝑑 ( 𝑝)}, minmax normalized Δ+ 𝑑 and Δ 𝑑 over candidate parcels within each dataset, and averaged them to obtain 𝐿𝑑 ( 𝑝) = 1 2 {(cid:101)Δ+ 𝑑 ( 𝑝) + (cid:101)Δ 𝑑 ( 𝑝)}. The rationale is that datasets in 𝐷(𝑐) instantiate capability 𝑐; therefore, if steering parcel 𝑝 systematically changes answer likelihood on those datasets, the parcel provides causal evidence for that capability beyond passive co-activation. Activation and intervention evidence were fused over the union of the top 50 activation-ranked and top 50 intervention-ranked parcels: 𝑆𝑑 ( 𝑝) = 𝛼 𝐴𝑑 ( 𝑝) + (1 𝛼) 𝐿𝑑 ( 𝑝), with 𝛼 = 0.5, where tildes denote minmax normalization over retained parcels within dataset. For capability 𝑐, dataset weights 𝑤𝑐,𝑑 were normalized across the matched datasets 𝐷(𝑐): ˆ𝑤𝑐,𝑑 = max(𝑤𝑐,𝑑, 0) (cid:205)𝑑 𝐷(𝑐) max(𝑤𝑐,𝑑 , 0) .

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: This normalization was applied when at least one matched dataset had positive weight; otherwise the matched datasets were assigned uniform weights. The data-driven capabilityparcel score was 𝑅𝑐 ( 𝑝) = 𝑑 𝐷(𝑐) ˆ𝑤𝑐,𝑑𝑆𝑑 ( 𝑝). Capability and parcel texts were embedded with Qwen3-Embedding-8B, and semantic consistency was defined as 𝑄𝑐 ( 𝑝) = cos(𝑒𝑐, 𝑒𝑝). The final signed capabilityparcel mapping was 𝑀 (𝑐, 𝑝) = (1 𝛽) 𝑧(𝑅𝑐 ( 𝑝)) + 𝛽 𝑧(𝑄𝑐 ( 𝑝)), with 𝛽 = 0.5, where 𝑧() denotes z-scoring across retained capabilityparcel pairs. Positive and negative associations were retained, corresponding respectively to supporting and comparatively suppressive relationships. Cross-model mappings from capabilities to parcels are reported in Supplementary Table 11. The signed mapping was used for atlas interpretation and capabilityparcel reporting. For pathology detection and trajectory aggregation, we derived separate non-negative parcel-by-capability matrix M+ ℝ𝑃𝐶 0 by taking the positive part of the signed mapping, transposing it to parcel-by-capability orientation and normalizing each capability column with positive parcel assignments: 𝑀+ 𝑝,𝑐 = max{𝑀 (𝑐, 𝑝), 0} (cid:205)𝑝 max{𝑀 (𝑐, 𝑝), 0} , Detector features therefore aggregated evidence from parcels positively assigned to capability, whereas comparatively suppressive associations were retained only in the signed atlas-level map. Capabilities were organized into four hierarchy levels: perception, representation, abstraction and application. Assignments were informed by semantic relations among capability definitions and Bloom-inspired distinctions in cognitive complexity, then reviewed for consistency with the operational definitions of the four levels. Capability-level definitions and assignments are reported in Supplementary Table 7.

sections.title: Validation of atlas organization
sections.content: We evaluated NeuroCogMap organization at the level of parcels, parcel descriptions, parcelcapability mappings and capability hierarchy. Semantic homogeneity of the functional atlas (Fig. 2a2) was tested using the ten highest-activating examples for each parcel. When an activated sentence was marked, only that sentence was embedded; otherwise, the full sample content was used. Texts were embedded with Qwen3-Embedding-8B and L2-normalized. Within-parcel similarity was the mean pairwise cosine similarity among selected texts, and between-parcel similarity was the mean similarity between texts in the parcel and selected texts from all other parcels. Withinand between-parcel similarities were compared across parcels using paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test. Cross-LLM correspondence and functional similarity of matched parcels (Fig. 2a3,b3) used parcel function descriptions to align source and target parcels. Candidate target parcels were ranked by Qwen3-Embedding8B semantic similarity; the five highest-scoring candidates were then evaluated by GPT-OSS-20B. The final alignment score was 𝐻 ( 𝑝, 𝑞) = 0.4 𝐸( 𝑝, 𝑞) + 0.6 𝐽 ( 𝑝, 𝑞), where 𝐸( 𝑝, 𝑞) is embedding cosine similarity rescaled to [0, 1], and 𝐽 ( 𝑝, 𝑞) is the LLM-judged functional similarity score on the same scale. For each matched pair and dataset with sufficient train and test samples, scalar linear regression model was fit on the training split and evaluated by Pearson correlation on the held-out split. Sentence-level examples were sampled from datasets shared by the source and target models with fixed random seeds for train and test selection. Random non-matched target parcels were used as baseline. Predictability of parcel activation from functional descriptions (Fig. 2b1) tested whether functional descriptions recovered empirical parcel activation rankings on held-out data. This analysis used the primary Gemma2-2B atlas containing 270 parcels and evaluated 40 held-out datasets with up to 500 examples per

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: dataset. Each example and parcel text was embedded with Qwen3-Embedding-8B [53]; the ten highestsimilarity parcels were then passed to GPT-OSS-20B [86], served through local vLLM endpoint [88], which assigned activation probabilities using temperature 0.3 and structured output. Ground-truth rankings were computed from held-out SAE activations after setting negative activations to zero. Prediction accuracy was quantified by Pearson correlation against these held-out empirical rankings. Baselines included random-clustering, neuron-based and keyword-only controls when available, and paired two-sided Wilcoxon signed-rank tests with Holm correction were performed across datasets. Targeted parcel intervention (Fig. 2b2) tested whether positive steering of an individual parcel shifted generated outputs towards that parcels assigned function. For each parcel, decoder directions for all assigned SAE latents were added at the corresponding residual-stream hook points during prompt processing and autoregressive decoding. Fig. 2b2 summarizes the factual question-answering examples sampled from HaluEval [29] for evaluation, with up to 100 examples assigned to each parcel. Inputs were truncated to 1,024 tokens, generations were capped at 128 new tokens and decoding was greedy. The primary scoring mode embedded the parcel description and the preand post-intervention responses with Qwen3-Embedding-8B and compared cosine similarity after rescaling to [0, 1]. GPT-OSS-20B scoring was retained as an optional judge-based check rather than the primary score. For multi-strength analyses, the post-intervention score for an example was the maximum score observed across the prespecified positive-strength grid; examples whose generated text did not change were excluded from the intervention-effect calculation. This maximum-over-strength summary was used only to evaluate parcel-function consistency, not to select the reported pathology-mitigation strength. Activation selectivity of parcelcapability links and performance change after perturbation (Fig. 2c1,c2) were tested by asking whether high-scoring links were selectively activated by the corresponding capability and whether perturbing linked parcels caused larger changes in answer log probability than perturbing unrelated controls. Correlation-based analyses used held-out test examples and SAE activations where available, comparing capability-relevant datasets with matched low-relevance datasets or comparing high-, lowand random-ranked parcel groups on capability-relevant datasets. Perturbation analyses used the same selected parcel groups and measured changes in the conditional log probability of answer tokens only, with inputs truncated to 1,024 tokens and up to 128 examples per dataset. Suppressive ablation used steering strength 1.0, and positive sufficiency steering used strength 0.5 in the main Fig. 2c summaries. Random controls were sampled after excluding the high-related parcels using fixed seed. Semantic organization across hierarchy layers (Fig. 2d1) was tested by asking whether parcels mapped to hierarchy level were semantically closer to their own level description than to other level descriptions. For each capability, the top ten ranked parcels were selected from the final Gemma2-2B capabilityparcel map; parcels were pooled within each hierarchy level by parcel identifier, sorted by recurrence across capabilities and truncated to at most 20 unique parcels per level. Parcel texts and four fixed hierarchy descriptions were embedded with Qwen3-Embedding-8B and compared by cosine similarity. Extended hierarchy analyses quantified semantic dispersion as one minus the mean pairwise cosine similarity among top-ranked parcel descriptions pooled by hierarchy level, and structural degree after thresholding the model-level parcelparcel connectome at the strongest 10% of off-diagonal absolute weights and summarizing it as an undirected, unweighted graph. Capability prerequisite learning across hierarchy layers (Fig. 2d2) was evaluated with controlled synthetic training experiments separating one-hop in-context retrieval, one-hop parametric recall and two-hop reasoning. This analysis tested whether estimated capability dependencies could predict staged learning dynamics in setting where prerequisite relations were controlled. The task design was inspired by controlled synthetic knowledge-storage experiments in the Physics of Language Models framework [89]. Synthetic person profiles contained name and five attributes: birth date, birth city, university, major and company. These profiles generated biography text, one-hop QA examples and two-hop relational questions; representative examples are provided in the Supplementary Information. GPT-NeoX/Pythia models at 70M, 160M and 410M scale were initialized from configuration rather than pretrained weights, so the experiment measured controlled synthetic training rather than continued pretraining. Models used AdamW [90], cosine learning-rate schedules, mixed precision, gradient clipping and gradient checkpointing. The 70M and 160M models were trained for 40,000 steps with 1,000 warmup steps, batch size 96, maximum learning rate 103 and minimum learning rate 104; the 410M model used 40,000 steps, 5,000 warmup steps, batch size 32 with gradient accumulation 4, maximum learning rate 5 105 and minimum learning rate 105. Held-out generation used temperature 0, top-𝑝 = 1 and maximum length 50, and response was counted as correct if it contained the gold answer string.
