sections.content: Plotted summaries used five repeated runs for 70M, 160M and 410M; pairwise comparisons used Welchs two-sample 𝑡-tests. Pathology analysis, detection and intervention Hallucination, social bias, refusal failure and sycophancy were analysed with common contrastive framework. Labels followed the original dataset annotations or evaluation protocols and were collapsed into normative and pathological conditions for the contrastive analyses: truthful versus hallucinated responses for factual answering, fair or unbiased versus biased responses for BBQ social-bias examples [91], successful refusal versus refusal failure for harmful-instruction prompts, and independent versus sycophantic responses for Sycophancy-Eval [92]. Hallucination labels were assigned by reference-based semantic correctness; BBQ responses were mapped to uncertainty, stereotype-aligned and non-stereotyped alternatives; refusal labels separated safe refusal from harmful compliance; and sycophancy labels separated independent answers from user-influenced shifts in Answer and Feedback settings. Dataset-specific roles, sample sizes and representative task patterns are provided in Supplementary Table 1 and the Supplementary Information. For each prompt-response pair, SAE activations were extracted over the generated answer span and mapped to parcels. Let X𝑖 ℝ𝑇𝑖 𝑃 denote the parcel trajectory for sample 𝑖, where 𝑇𝑖 is the number of answer tokens and 𝑃 is the number of parcels. The sample-level parcel activation vector was a𝑖 = 1 𝑇𝑖 𝑇𝑖 𝑡=1 X𝑖,𝑡. Capability trajectories were obtained with the non-negative parcelcapability matrix M+ ℝ𝑃𝐶 0 , Y𝑖 = X𝑖M+, c𝑖 = 1 𝑇𝑖 𝑇𝑖 𝑡=1 Y𝑖,𝑡. Parcel and capability functional connectivity matrices were computed from answer-token trajectories as F𝑖 = FisherZ(corr𝑡 (X𝑖)) , G𝑖 = FisherZ(corr𝑡 (Y𝑖)) . Diagonal entries were excluded from edge-level analyses. Pathology signatures were defined as normativeminus-pathological contrasts over the normative set and pathological set P, Δa = aN aP , Δc = cN cP , ΔF = FN FP , ΔG = GN GP . Under this convention, positive contrasts indicate stronger activation or connectivity in normative responses, whereas negative contrasts indicate stronger activation or connectivity in pathological responses. Hierarchy-level summaries averaged capability contrasts within each hierarchy level ℎ, Δℎ = 𝐶ℎ1 Δc,𝑐. 𝑐𝐶ℎ Edge-, parceland capability-level contrasts were tested across samples. For detection, features were constructed from the generated answer span, so the detector was evaluated as response-level classifier of completed generations rather than as pre-generation monitor. Features were derived from the parcel activation vector, parcel functional-connectivity matrix, capability activation vector and capability functional-connectivity matrix. Pathology-relevant signatures were selected within each training fold by prespecified top-𝑘 rule applied to training-fold contrastive statistics: up to ten normative-shifted parcels, ten pathology-shifted parcels, ten normative-shifted capabilities, ten pathology-shifted capabilities and the top 25 normativeand pathology-shifted differential edges at the parcel and capability connectivity levels. The selected signature indices were then applied unchanged to the held-out fold. Hierarchy-level profiles were used for interpretation and visualization but not as an additional classifier feature block. Each response was represented by 12-dimensional interpretable feature vector. Let 𝑃+, 𝑃, 𝐶+ and 𝐶 denote the training-fold parcel and capability sets with positive and negative normative-minus-pathological

The final four prototype-alignment features compared the response with normative and pathological trainingfold prototypes: 𝑥9 = cos(a𝑖, 𝜇 𝑥11 = 𝜙(F𝑖, 𝜇 ) cos(a𝑖, 𝜇 ) 𝜙(F𝑖, 𝜇 ), ), 𝑥10 = cos(c𝑖, 𝜇 𝑥12 = 𝜙(G𝑖, 𝜇 ) cos(c𝑖, 𝜇 ) 𝜙(G𝑖, 𝜇 ), ), where 𝜙( 𝐴, 𝐵) = 1/(1 + 𝐴 𝐵 𝐹). Together, these features comprise four activation indicators, four connectivitymismatch scores and four prototype-alignment differences. To avoid adaptive model selection, all reported NeuroCogMap detectors used the same logistic-regression classifier, implemented with L2 regularization and class-balanced weights. Evaluation was performed separately for each pathology dataset and model using stratified fivefold crossvalidation with shuffle enabled and random seed 42. Signature selection, normative and pathological prototypes, feature standardization and logistic-regression fitting were estimated within the training fold only. AUROC was the primary detection metric and was computed from held-out predicted probabilities. Pathology-specific baselines used the same labels and cross-validation splits and are described in the Supplementary Information. Mechanism-guided mitigation converted significant signed parcel abnormalities into generation-time steering targets. Under the normative-minus-pathological convention, positive parcels were treated as enhancement targets because they were under-recruited in pathological responses, whereas negative parcels were treated as suppression targets because they were over-recruited in pathological responses. We therefore selected the three most positive significant parcels for enhancement and the three most negative significant parcels for suppression. If fewer significant parcels were available in either direction, all available significant parcels in that direction were used. Let denote the selected steering-target set and let Δ𝑝 Δa,𝑝 denote the parcel-level activation contrast for parcel 𝑝. For each 𝑝 , the signed steering coefficient was 𝛼𝑝 = 𝜆steer tanh(Δ𝑝/𝑠), Δ𝑝 > 0, 𝜆steer tanh(Δ𝑝/𝑠), Δ𝑝 < 0, Δ𝑝 = 0, 0, with 𝜆steer = 0.3 and 𝑠 = 80. For each latent 𝑘 in parcel 𝑝 at layer ℓ, the steering direction was the SAE decoder vector scaled by the maximum activation observed for that latent. During generation, all selected parcel directions were applied additively: ℎℓ,𝑡 ℎℓ,𝑡 + 𝜂 𝛼𝑝 𝑚ℓ𝑘𝑊dec ℓ𝑘 . 𝑝 𝑘𝐹 𝑝,ℓ The intervention-strength grid was 𝜂 {0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0}. For each dataset, the steering strength was selected on an independent validation split using the task-specific evaluation metric and then fixed before evaluation on the held-out test split. Baseline and steered generations used the same prompts and were evaluated with the same task-specific evaluator. Unless otherwise specified, generation used greedy decoding with temperature 0 and maximum of 128 new tokens.

sections.title: Human cortical prediction and correspondence
sections.content: HumanLLM correspondence was evaluated with the LeBel story-listening fMRI dataset, in which eight participants listened to extended autobiographical narratives. Analyses used released preprocessed cortical responses, projected to the Schaefer100 parcellation and summarized by Yeo seven-network assignments. Participants were included only if their mean framewise displacement was below 0.2 mm. NeuroCogMap features were extracted from Gemma2-2B with the 270-parcel atlas. Model inputs used 256-token preceding context. NeuroCogMap features were downsampled to fMRI repetition times and expanded with finite impulse response delays. Feature and response matrices were standardized within story and participant. Ridge-regression encoding models predicted held-out cortical responses from all NeuroCogMap parcels jointly. Following the published evaluation protocol [47], the final story, Where Theres Smoke, was held out for evaluation. Ridge regularization was selected by inner cross-validation on training stories over 101 to 108, separately for each cortical target. Prediction accuracy was Pearson correlation between predicted and observed held-out responses, Fisher-𝑧-transformed for averaging and back-transformed for reporting. Baselines included WordRate, Word2Vec, BERT base uncased, Gemma2-2B Language Context and Gemma2-2B Language Standard. All baselines were implemented within the same encoding framework [50], using identical story splits, temporal downsampling, finite impulse response delays, standardization, ridge fitting and held-out evaluation; only the input feature representation was replaced. The NeuroCogMap representation used only 270 parcel-level predictors, providing compact, low-dimensional encoding space relative to hidden-state language baselines, which used substantially higher-dimensional model representations such as 2,304-dimensional Gemma2-2B hidden-state features before temporal expansion. Thus, its prediction performance reflects the ability of functionally organized parcels to capture brain-relevant variance with comparatively small set of interpretable predictors. Functional correspondence was tested by comparing the cognitive profile of each cortical parcel with the NeuroCogMap parcels that best predicted it. Each Schaefer100 parcel was assigned cognitive profile from Neurosynth meta-analytic associations with 123 Cognitive Atlas terms. The 50 terms with the largest absolute 𝑧-scores were converted into short functional description containing function name, 100200 word description and role summary. For each human parcel, top-ten and bottom-ten NeuroCogMap parcels were selected according to held-out prediction accuracy. Similarities between human and NeuroCogMap descriptions were computed with Qwen3-Embedding-8B, averaged within each human parcel and summarized within Yeo networks. complementary GPT-OSS-20B judge assigned functional-correspondence scores in [0, 1] using six cognitive-domain criteria. The case-study panel used the held-out story Where Theres Smoke; candidate correspondences were selected from high prediction-ranked matches and manually curated only to cover dorsal-attention, default-mode, control-monitoring and social-affective systems, not as an independent statistical test. For representational similarity analysis, human similarity matrices were constructed from human functional descriptions and NeuroCogMap matrices from the mean embedding of the top three predictionranked NeuroCogMap descriptions for each human parcel. Matrices were constructed within Salience/Ventral Attention, Frontoparietal Control and Default networks, separately by hemisphere, and upper-triangular entries were compared with Pearson correlation. For RSA, BenjaminiHochberg correction was applied across the six networkhemisphere comparisons. Prompt templates for cortical-profile description and humanLLM functional-correspondence judgement are provided in the Supplementary prompt templates section.

sections.title: Cognitively grounded model discovery
sections.content: Model-discovery analyses tested whether NeuroCogMap features could guide refinement of classical cognitive models. We first evaluated whether Gemma2-2B NeuroCogMap parcel activations predicted trial-wise fMRI responses in public two-step decision-making dataset [55]. The released Schaefer100 ROI table contained 94 participants, comprising 48 participants in the abstract task version and 46 in the story task version. For the neural-alignment analysis, ten participants were selected by task-version stratification and coverage of the model-based to model-free behavioural spectrum, with six from the abstract version and four from the story version. Included participants had at least two fMRI runs and at least 140 valid trials. The selected subset spanned model-based behavioural index from 1.468 to 1.577 and fitted model-based weights from 0.000 to 0.905. For the neural-prediction analysis, each decision trial was represented by the text prompt and recent trial

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: history used for Gemma2-2B activation extraction. Trial-wise beta estimates from Schaefer100 ROIs were predicted from the resulting 270-dimensional NeuroCogMap parcel-activation features. Within each participant and cross-validation fold, all ROI targets were regressed jointly in multivariate ridge fit. The ridge penalty was selected by nested leave-one-run-out cross-validation over scanning blocks. Inputs were standardized and reduced by principal component analysis retaining 95% of training-set variance before ridge fitting. Prediction accuracy was computed separately for each ROI and averaged across ROIs to obtain one participant-level score. Participant-level scores were Fisher-𝑧-transformed for aggregation, and 95% confidence intervals were computed by bootstrap resampling participants. We evaluated five representative cognitive experiments from Psych-101: episodic long-term memory, multiattribute decision-making, Shepard categorization, intertemporal choice and drifting four-armed bandit; task structures and behavioural sample sizes are summarized in Supplementary Table 5. Gemma2-2B was treated as behavioural simulator by assigning likelihoods to candidate human responses from trial-wise prompts. Participant-level negative log-likelihood was converted to AIC as AIC = 2𝑘 + 2NLL, where 𝑘 is the number of fitted parameters and NLL is the summed negative log-likelihood over trials. Lower AIC indicates better fit. Parcel-wise Pearson correlations between participant-level NeuroCogMap activation and AIC were used to identify beneficial and interfering contributors, with negative correlations indicating better fit. For the predictive analysis in Fig. 6b, participant-level 270-dimensional NeuroCogMap activation vectors were used to predict participant-level AIC using the predefined traintest partitions provided with the Psych-101 datasets. multivariate linear model was fit on the training participants and evaluated on the held-out test participants; the reported metric was the held-out Pearson correlation between predicted and observed AIC, tested two-sided against 𝜌 = 0. Model discovery focused on the two-step task because it is classic and mechanistically rich sequential decision-making paradigm with an established Dual-systems Model benchmark. Following the Centaur openloop protocol, we used the Psych-101 test-participant split to define held-out participants for each twostep dataset; these participants were excluded from model discovery, and the remaining participants in the corresponding original two-step dataset formed the discovery set. NeuroCogMap summaries, LLM-favoured participant selection and candidate model generation used only discovery-set participants. This analysis builds on recent model-guided scientific discovery work around Psych-101, including the Centaur multi-attribute decision-making study, in which foundation-model behavioural predictions were used to guide the refinement of interpretable cognitive models [58]. We extended this paradigm by supplementing behavioural traces, residuals and AIC comparisons with NeuroCogMap-derived parceland capability-level summaries of the models internal organization. Behavioural regimes were treated as LLM-favoured when the LLM simulator had lower AIC than the classical model, with no additional margin. reasoning agent using GPT-5.2 [87] with temperature 0.7 proposed candidate cognitive mechanisms, and model-writing agent converted these mechanisms into executable model extensions. The NeuroCogMap-guided and behaviour-only branches used the same reasoning agent, model-writing agent, search budget and screening protocol; the behaviour-only branch received no NeuroCogMap features. Human checking was limited to faithfulness to the proposed mechanisms, executable syntax, fit compatibility and removal of unsupported claims, and did not substantively rewrite candidate model proposals. The baseline model for the two-step model-discovery analysis was the standard Dual-systems Model [61] for the two-step task. This classical baseline was used as the reference model against which the behaviour-only and NeuroCogMap-guided extensions were compared. In the implementation used for AIC computation, the original Dual-systems Model contained 5 trainable parameters, the behaviour-only discovered extension contained 18 trainable parameters, and the NeuroCogMap-guided extension contained 24 trainable parameters. The behaviour-only extension included lapse and omission terms, prediction-error-dependent arbitration, state-specific second-stage win-stay/lose-shift and perseveration, value forgetting, post-omission reset and adaptive learning-rate terms. The AIC penalty used these implementation-level parameter counts for each participant-level fit. After candidate generation, the selected model classes were fixed before evaluation on held-out Psych-101 test participants from Two Step Task One and Two Step Task Two [56, 57]. Two Step Task One contained 51,500 trials from 206 participants and Two Step Task Two contained 40,000 trials from 100 participants. Following the Centaur open-loop fitting protocol, the original Dual-systems Model, behaviour-only discovered model and NeuroCogMap-discovered model were refit separately for each held-out participant using that participants complete choice sequence; participant-level AIC was computed from the summed negative log-likelihood over the same sequence. Aggregate AIC was computed as the mean participant-level AIC. Fig. 6d is reported as held-out participant-level model-comparison evidence rather than as separate inferential test.

sections.title: Statistical analysis and implementation details
sections.content: Detailed test statistics, effect sizes and panel-specific sample sizes are reported in the Results, figure legends and Supplementary Tables. In the Methods, we specify the unit of analysis and inferential family for each analysis to support reproducibility. Unless otherwise stated, generated responses were the unit for pathology analyses, parcels or capabilities were the unit for atlas-validation comparisons, cortical parcels were the unit for human encoding comparisons, and participants were the unit for behavioural model comparisons. Paired tests were used for matched designs, Welch tests for independent groups with unequal variances and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests for paired non-parametric comparisons. Multiple comparisons were controlled with Holm-Bonferroni or BenjaminiHochberg correction [94, 95] as specified for the corresponding analysis. Error bars denote the uncertainty measure specified in each figure legend: 95% confidence intervals were computed over the plotted analysis units unless explicitly described as bootstrap intervals, standard deviations summarize repeated runs or cross-validation folds where stated, and Fig. 6a uses participant-bootstrap confidence intervals. All experiments were implemented in Python. Model inference, activation extraction and parcel interventions used PyTorch-based code with SAE Lens and TransformerLens-compatible hooks. Numerical analysis used NumPy, SciPy, pandas, scikit-learn and sentence-transformers. Random seeds were fixed at 42 for stochastic dimensionality reduction, random controls, cross-validation splits and sampling-based validation procedures unless otherwise stated. Experiments were run on Linux server with eight NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPUs, dual Intel Xeon Gold 6346 CPUs, NVIDIA driver 535.274.02 and CUDA 12.2. Exact software and dependency versions, together with environment-reconstruction instructions, are provided in the public code repository and its archived release.

sections.title: Supplementary contents
sections.content: Aggregate category analysis of pathology-related activation differences Social bias reflects misrouting of socially salient representations Mechanism-guided detection and mitigation of social bias Sycophancy reflects distributed weakening of independent judgment Mechanism-guided detection and mitigation of sycophancy Pathology datasets and representative task patterns Pathology detection baselines Cross-model safety-pathology detection and intervention in Llama-3.1-8B Cross-model NeuroCogMap atlas and parcelcapability mapping summaries Parcel construction is stable across clustering hyperparameters Capability-associated parcels show hierarchical semantic and structural organization Synthetic examples for hierarchy-dependency validation Psych-101 cognitive-task datasets used in Fig. 6b Human evaluation of LLM-assisted NeuroCogMap components NeuroCogMap supplementary atlas and dataset resources Supplementary prompt templates 37 37 40 40 42 42 44 49 50 50 50 53 54 55 58 75 Aggregate category analysis of pathology-related activation differences We further examined whether pathological behaviours differed in the category of NeuroCogMap units they perturbed. Parcels and capabilities were grouped as belief-related (representation-related) or behaviouralcontrol-related, and we compared the absolute activation difference between pathological and non-pathological examples for each group (Extended Data Fig. 7). Hallucination and bias showed larger activation differences in belief-related parcels and capabilities, consistent with their interpretation as representational pathologies involving disrupted semantic grounding, knowledge integration or internal consistency. Refusal failure and sycophancy showed the opposite pattern at the parcel level, with larger activation differences in behaviouralcontrol-related units. The corresponding capability-level effects were directionally consistent but weaker and did not reach significance for either behavioural-control pathology. Thus, the category-level analysis supports separation between representational and behavioural-control failure modes, while indicating that this separation is most clearly resolved at the finer parcel granularity.

sections.title: Social bias reflects misrouting of socially salient representations
sections.content: We next examined social bias in BBQ [69, 91], focusing first on the disability subset by comparing fair and biased responses. NeuroCogMap revealed representational pathology distinct from hallucination. Rather than reflecting failed truth evaluation or disrupted factual grounding, biased responses were characterized by abnormal coupling between demographic representations and knowledge-retrieval pathways, whereas fair responses preferentially recruited structured comparison, neutral inference and higher-order normative control (Extended Data Fig. 8ad). At the circuit level, biased responses were dominated by demographic-retrieval pathway centred on Demographic Comparison. The strongest bias-enhanced edges linked Celebrity Knowledge Retrieval to Demographic Comparison (Δ𝑤 = 8.68, 𝑃 = 1.8 1024), Wikipedia Fact Retrieval to Demographic Comparison (Δ𝑤 = 8.68, 𝑃 = 9.3 1035), and Fact Extraction Module to Demographic Comparison (Δ𝑤 = 6.91, 𝑃 = 2.6 1017). Biased responses also showed stronger coupling involving the Neutral Inference Module, including Factual Retrieval ModuleNeutral Inference Module (Δ𝑤 = 7.28, 𝑃 = 3.7 1018) and Neutral Inference ModuleDemographic Comparison (Δ𝑤 = 7.34, 𝑃 = 1.1 1016). This pattern suggests that bias was not best explained by lack of relevant knowledge, but by the way socially salient category information was coupled to factual and encyclopaedic retrieval. Fair responses, by contrast, strengthened structured comparison-and-uncertainty circuit, including Factoid Retrieval ModuleItem Enumeration (Δ𝑤 = 7.80, 𝑃 < 10300), Spatial Direction Processing Locative Retrieval Module (Δ𝑤 = 7.29, 𝑃 = 1.1 1032), and Number-Retrieval ModuleLocative Retrieval Module

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: Figure 7 Category-level activation differences across representational and behavioural-control pathologies. a,b, Absolute parceland capability-level activation differences for two representational pathologies: hallucination (a) and bias (b). For hallucination, activation contrasts were computed between truthful and hallucinated outputs; for bias, contrasts were computed between fair or unbiased and biased outputs. c,d, The same analysis for two behavioural-control pathologies: refusal failure (c) and sycophancy (d). For refusal failure, contrasts were computed between successful refusal and refusal-failed outputs; for sycophancy, contrasts were computed between independent and sycophantic outputs. Capabilities were defined from fixed taxonomy of interpretable functional competencies, and parcels were defined as functionally annotated sparse-autoencoder regions of residual-stream activations. Each capability definition and parcel functional summary was classified under fixed prompt-based rubric as belief-related (representation-related), behavioural-control-related, mixed, neutral or unknown (see the Supplementary prompt templates section). Belief-related units corresponded to functions associated with semantic grounding, knowledge integration and internal consistency, whereas behavioural-control-related units corresponded to inhibition, arbitration among competing objectives and output regulation. Only units assigned to the belief-related or behavioural-control-related categories were retained for the two-group comparison shown here; mixed, neutral and unknown units were excluded. For each retained parcel or capability, the plotted value denotes the absolute activation contrast between the two conditions, Δactivation, so that larger values indicate stronger differential engagement irrespective of direction. Unit-level contrasts were aggregated across included benchmarks by taking the maximum absolute contrast across datasets. Violin plots show the distribution across units, overlaid points denote individual parcels or capabilities, and dashed horizontal lines denote zero activation difference. 𝑃 values were computed using two-sample Welch 𝑡-tests comparing belief-related and behavioural-control-related groups; significance stars denote conventional thresholds. (Δ𝑤 = 7.28, 𝑃 < 10300). Fair responses also showed stronger control from Number-Retrieval Module and Locative Retrieval Module to Uncertainty Reasoner (Δ𝑤 = 7.10, 𝑃 < 10300; Δ𝑤 = 6.97, 𝑃 = 1.5 1043). Thus, NeuroCogMap separates two circuit regimes: bias-prone route in which demographic representations are directly linked to retrieval pathways, and fair-response route in which the model relies more on comparison, enumeration and uncertainty-sensitive reasoning (Extended Data Fig. 8a). Parcel-level activity reinforced this interpretation. Fair responses strongly recruited parcels supporting explicit comparison and structured evaluation, including Duality Coordination (Δ𝑎 = 187.24, 𝑃 = 2.6 10298), Universal Claim Processor (Δ𝑎 = 111.98, 𝑃 = 4.0 10297), Number-Retrieval Module (Δ𝑎 = 78.74, 𝑃 = 1.910211), Universal Attribute Reasoning (Δ𝑎 = 49.63, 𝑃 = 5.110138), Neutral Inference Module (Δ𝑎 = 44.97, 𝑃 = 5.3 1047) and Social Norm Reasoning (Δ𝑎 = 34.42, 𝑃 = 6.8 107). Biased responses instead overrecruited socially loaded or category-linked factual parcels, including Medical Disease Knowledge (Δ𝑎 = 55.61, 𝑃 = 3.9 107), Demographic Comparison (Δ𝑎 = 47.49, 𝑃 = 4.6 105), Professional Knowledge Retrieval (Δ𝑎 = 38.29, 𝑃 = 7.7 106), Key-Term Retrieval (Δ𝑎 = 30.59, 𝑃 = 3.0 105), Factual Retrieval Module (Δ𝑎 = 23.00, 𝑃 = 5.5 1015) and Hate-Speech and Profanity Detector (Δ𝑎 = 18.40, 𝑃 = 0.0014). In the disability domain, the co-activation of Medical Disease Knowledge and Demographic Comparison suggests specific failure mode: clinically or socially associated category information was treated as relevant evidence for answering, rather than being recognized as potentially stereotype-laden cue requiring control (Extended Data Fig. 8b). At the capability level, fair responses preferentially recruited high-level evaluative and alignment-related capacities, including Causal reasoning (Δ𝑐 = 19.92, 𝑃 = 1.1 1068), Comprehension and interpretation

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: Figure 8 NeuroCogMap identifies, detects and mitigates representational pathologies underlying social-bias in LLMs. ad, Multi-level NeuroCogMap signatures separating unbiased from biased responses, including anomalous parcelparcel connectivity (a), parcel activation shifts (b), capability activation shifts (c) and hierarchy-level activation differences (d). All contrasts use an unbiasedminus-biased sign; for connectivity, positive values indicate stronger unbiased-response connectivity and negative values indicate stronger biased-response connectivity. e, Bias detection from NeuroCogMap-derived pathology signatures, measured by AUROC across BBQ subsets in Gemma2-2B and Gemma2-9B-IT. Insets show average AUROC across subsets. f, Bias mitigation by NeuroCogMap-guided parcel intervention, measured by fairness accuracy, defined as the unbiased-response rate. Unless otherwise stated, analyses were performed on Gemma2-2B. Error bars indicate 95% confidence intervals in b,c and standard deviation in e. Statistical significance in b,c was assessed using two-sided paired 𝑡-tests: *𝑃 < 0.05, **𝑃 < 0.01, ***𝑃 < 0.001. Dataset details and sample sizes are reported in Methods and Supplementary Table 1; intervention procedures are described in Methods. (Δ𝑐 = 13.63, 𝑃 = 2.2 1054), Induction and inference (Δ𝑐 = 11.28, 𝑃 = 1.3 1045), Analytical thinking (Δ𝑐 = 10.27, 𝑃 = 7.2 1042), Ethical reasoning (Δ𝑐 = 8.22, 𝑃 = 0.0015), Alignment (Δ𝑐 = 8.18, 𝑃 = 9.5 106), Bias mitigation (Δ𝑐 = 7.33, 𝑃 = 1.1 105), Judgment (Δ𝑐 = 6.15, 𝑃 = 6.0 107) and Verification (Δ𝑐 = 4.29, 𝑃 = 4.4 104). Biased responses were relatively stronger in Common sense reasoning (Δ𝑐 = 9.72, 𝑃 = 2.7 105), Knowledge recall (Δ𝑐 = 5.63, 𝑃 = 3.0 1015), Attention (Δ𝑐 = 5.50, 𝑃 = 2.5 108), Information retrieval (Δ𝑐 = 4.49, 𝑃 = 4.3 1012), Knowledge integration (Δ𝑐 = 4.27, 𝑃 = 5.6 1010) and Knowledge extraction (Δ𝑐 = 4.25, 𝑃 = 6.1 1010). This capability-level dissociation shows that biased responses were not simply less capable responses. Rather, they reflected shift from controlled evaluation and normative judgment towards more automatic regime dominated by common-sense association, attention and knowledge retrieval (Extended Data Fig. 8c). At the hierarchy level, biased responses showed relatively greater activation in Perceptual Access and Attentional Gating, whereas fair responses shifted towards Abstract Reasoning and Meta-Cognitive Control and Situated Application and Social Interaction. This pattern suggests that social bias reflects an imbalance between early access to socially salient category information and higher-order processes that evaluate, compare and regulate its relevance for the response. Thus, whereas hallucination primarily involved failures in how factual knowledge was retrieved, monitored or coordinated, bias involved distinct representational failure in how social information was routed into normative and context-sensitive decision processes (Extended Data Fig. 8d). Together, these findings show that social bias in LLMs is not merely superficial output preference. NeuroCogMap reveals systems-level representational pathology in which socially salient category information becomes over-coupled to factual retrieval and common-sense association, while comparison, uncertainty-sensitive reasoning and normative evaluation are insufficiently recruited. This distinguishes bias from hallucination: both involve representational failure, but bias arises from how social representations are routed and regulated, rather than from general failure of truth evaluation or factual grounding.

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: Mechanism-guided detection and mitigation of social bias Finally, we asked whether the bias-related pathology signatures identified by NeuroCogMap could also support diagnosis and intervention. Using the same mechanism-guided framework as above, we constructed detector from circuit-, parceland capability-level bias signatures and compared it with attention probing, hidden probing and perplexity baselines across four BBQ subdomains: Age, Disability, Gender and Nationality (Extended Data Fig. 8e). NeuroCogMap achieved near-ceiling discrimination in both models. On Gemma2-2B, it reached mean AUROC of 1.000, slightly exceeding hidden probing (0.999) and substantially outperforming perplexity (0.883) and attention probing (0.534). On Gemma2-9B-IT, NeuroCogMap again achieved near-ceiling performance (mean AUROC, 0.992), remaining above hidden probing (0.991) and outperforming perplexity (0.733) and attention probing (0.569). These results indicate that biased outputs are better captured by structured parcel-level pathology than by generic salience or surprisal measures. We then derived dataset-specific steering policies from the bias pathology map and tested whether these signatures contributed to biased response formation (Extended Data Fig. 8f). On Gemma2-2B, NeuroCogMapguided intervention produced substantial gains in fairness accuracy, increasing performance from 58.3% to 90.1% on BBQ-Age, from 60.4% to 65.6% on BBQ-Nationality, from 58.2% to 93.7% on BBQ-Gender, and from 56.2% to 88.8% on BBQ-Disability. Averaged across subdomains, fairness accuracy increased from 58.3% to 84.5%. On Gemma2-9B-IT, baseline fairness was already higher and intervention effects were smaller and mixed: fairness accuracy increased from 80.8% to 81.6% on BBQ-Age and from 89.6% to 91.6% on BBQ-Nationality, but decreased slightly from 88.9% to 88.5% on BBQ-Gender and from 88.4% to 84.8% on BBQ-Disability. These results suggest that NeuroCogMap-based steering is most effective when substantial residual bias pathology remains, whereas effects are limited and may be neutral or negative when baseline fairness is already high. Together, the hallucination and bias analyses indicate that representational pathologies in LLMs are structured but not unitary disturbances of internal cognitive organization. Hallucination disrupted factual retrieval, evaluation and cross-system coordination, whereas bias altered the routing of socially salient information into retrieval and normative-control pathways. Because these multilevel signatures were predictive and steerable under parcel intervention, NeuroCogMap links systems-level interpretation to mechanism-guided diagnosis and intervention.

sections.title: Sycophancy reflects distributed weakening of independent judgment
sections.content: We next examined sycophancy as second behavioural-control pathology, focusing first on the Feedback setting of Sycophancy-Eval by comparing independent and sycophantic responses. In contrast to refusal failure, which reflected shift into procedural action generation, sycophancy was characterized by broader attenuation of independent evaluative processing. Across circuit, parcel, capability and hierarchy levels, independent responses showed stronger recruitment of normative reasoning, contradiction-sensitive control and self-regulatory decision mechanisms, whereas sycophantic responses showed greater reliance on socially compliant or externally guided processing (Extended Data Fig. 9ad). At the circuit level, independent responses were supported by control-oriented network linking moral evaluation, contradiction analysis and self-regulatory decision processes. Legal-Moral Evaluation coupled more strongly with the Decision-Initiation Module (Δ𝑤 = 0.28, 𝑃 = 4.2 1015) and with Moral Lesson Detector (Δ𝑤 = 0.16, 𝑃 = 8.7 1021), while Moral Lesson Detector further coupled with Decision-Initiation Module (Δ𝑤 = 0.16, 𝑃 = 2.4 1024). Independent responses also showed stronger Causal ExtractionContradiction Analysis coupling (Δ𝑤 = 0.15, 𝑃 = 5.8 1028), together with inhibitory control linking Legal-Moral Evaluation and Decision-Initiation to Self-Refusal Detection (Δ𝑤 = 0.17, 𝑃 = 1.6 1025; Δ𝑤 = 0.13, 𝑃 = 1.1 1020). This pattern suggests that independent responding is actively maintained by circuits that detect conflict, evaluate normative implications and suppress premature agreement when correction is warranted. Sycophantic responses, by contrast, strengthened pathways associated with obedience and self-referential social cues, together with stronger inhibitory links involving contradiction-sensitive retrieval pathways. These included Self-Pride RecognitionHarassment Detection Module (Δ𝑤 = 0.26, 𝑃 < 10300), Narrative Media ComprehensionListening and Obedience Processing (Δ𝑤 = 0.20, 𝑃 < 10300), and inhibitory links from Contradiction Analysis to Factoid Retrieval (Δ𝑤 = 0.33, 𝑃 = 3.0 1041) and from Geopolitical-Trivia Retrieval to Numeric Extraction and Arithmetic (Δ𝑤 = 0.31, 𝑃 = 5.2 1017). Thus, sycophancy did not primarily reflect

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: Figure 9 NeuroCogMap identifies, detects and mitigates behavioural-control pathologies underlying sycophancy in LLMs. d, Multi-level NeuroCogMap signatures in the Sycophancy-Eval Feedback setting separating independent from sycophantic responses, including anomalous parcelparcel connectivity (a), parcel activation shifts (b), capability activation shifts (c) and hierarchy-level activation differences (d). Positive values indicate stronger activation or connectivity in independent responses; negative values indicate stronger activation or connectivity in sycophantic responses. e, Sycophancy detection from NeuroCogMap-derived pathology signatures, measured by AUROC in the Answer and Feedback settings in Gemma2-2B and Gemma2-9B-IT. f, Sycophancy mitigation by NeuroCogMap-guided parcel intervention, measured by sycophancy rate, where lower values indicate better mitigation. Unless otherwise stated, analyses were performed on Gemma2-2B. Error bars indicate 95% confidence intervals in b,c and standard deviation in e. Statistical significance in b,c,e was assessed using two-sided paired 𝑡-tests: *𝑃 < 0.05, **𝑃 < 0.01, ***𝑃 < 0.001; ns, not significant. Dataset details and sample sizes are reported in Methods and Supplementary Table 1; intervention procedures are described in Methods. stronger reasoning in favour of the users position, but shift in control state in which contradiction-sensitive retrieval was disrupted and socially receptive pathways gained influence (Extended Data Fig. 9a). Parcel-level activity showed consistent pattern. Independent responses more strongly activated Social Norm Reasoning (Δ𝑎 = 13.98, 𝑃 = 4.2 1032), Combinatorial Reasoning (Δ𝑎 = 12.50, 𝑃 = 0.0053), Legal-Moral Evaluation (Δ𝑎 = 4.51, 𝑃 = 6.41023), Modular Arithmetic Reasoner (Δ𝑎 = 4.28, 𝑃 = 0.0042) and Risk-Causality Analysis (Δ𝑎 = 3.93, 𝑃 = 4.7 1012). These parcels indicate that independence is not passive absence of agreement, but an actively maintained configuration involving social-norm evaluation, structured reasoning, risk evaluation and moral judgment. Sycophantic responses instead showed stronger activation of Uncertainty Clarification Module (Δ𝑎 = 12.68, 𝑃 = 1.0 1071), Mathematical Reasoning Core (Δ𝑎 = 7.47, 𝑃 = 0.0020), Logical Reasoning Core (Δ𝑎 = 3.90, 𝑃 = 1.9 1060), Self-Pride Recognition (Δ𝑎 = 2.57, 𝑃 = 2.4 1071) and Application Knowledge Hub (Δ𝑎 = 2.49, 𝑃 = 2.9 108). The recruitment of Self-Pride Recognition is particularly informative: sycophantic outputs appeared to increase sensitivity to user stance or self-image, rather than strengthening modules that adjudicate whether the stance is correct. NeuroCogMap therefore reveals sycophancy as reweighting of internal processing towards interpersonal alignment at the expense of independent evaluation (Extended Data Fig. 9b). At the capability level, independent responses consistently showed stronger activation of Fairness (Δ𝑐 = 2.81, 𝑃 = 4.2 1011), Ethical reasoning (Δ𝑐 = 2.74, 𝑃 = 1.9 107), Judgment (Δ𝑐 = 1.44, 𝑃 = 3.3 1026), Safety (Δ𝑐 = 1.09, 𝑃 = 1.2 104) and Verification (Δ𝑐 = 1.04, 𝑃 = 5.6 1022). By contrast, sycophantic responses were relatively stronger in Common sense reasoning (Δ𝑐 = 0.61, 𝑃 = 3.0 1013) and Logical reasoning (Δ𝑐 = 0.38, 𝑃 = 5.2 106). This dissociation suggests that sycophancy is unlikely to reflect simple loss of reasoning. Instead, socially independent judgment and verification are selectively weakened while lower-level inferential competence remains available, producing responses that favour agreement over correction (Extended Data Fig. 9c). At the hierarchy level, independent responses were stronger across the four NeuroCogMap layers, with the

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: largest separation in Perceptual Access and Attentional Gating and Situated Application and Social Interaction. This indicates that sycophancy is not confined to single high-level decision module. Rather, it reflects distributed attenuation of independent processing, beginning with how socially relevant cues are attended to and extending to how final socially situated responses are selected. The application-layer difference suggests that the ultimate pathology lies in socially conditioned behavioural output, whereas the additional reduction at lower levels suggests that user stance may already bias the initial framing of the interaction before explicit evaluation is complete (Extended Data Fig. 9d). Together, these findings distinguish sycophancy from refusal failure. Refusal failure reflects the release of procedural execution when harmful instructions escape control, whereas sycophancy reflects distributed weakening of independent evaluative control in favour of socially aligned response selection. NeuroCogMap therefore characterizes sycophancy as behavioural-control pathology in which conflict monitoring, normative evaluation and self-corrective judgment fail to maintain epistemic independence. Mechanism-guided detection and mitigation of sycophancy Finally, we asked whether the sycophancy-related signatures identified by NeuroCogMap could support diagnosis and intervention. Using the same mechanism-guided framework, we constructed detector from NeuroCogMapderived pathology signatures and evaluated it in the Answer and Feedback settings (Extended Data Fig. 9e). On Gemma2-2B, NeuroCogMap achieved AUROC values of 0.685 for Answer and 0.646 for Feedback, exceeding Perplexity (0.571 and 0.543), Self-Consistency (0.651 and 0.539) and User-Conditioned Attention (0.647 and 0.582). On Gemma2-9B-IT, NeuroCogMap again performed best overall, with AUROC values of 0.617 for Answer and 0.696 for Feedback, compared with Perplexity (0.595 and 0.523), Self-Consistency (0.479 and 0.560) and User-Conditioned Attention (0.560 and 0.649). These results indicate that sycophancy is better detected from distributed control abnormalities than from generic likelihood, sampled-response consistency or local attention to user-conditioning cues alone. We then derived steering policies from the sycophancy pathology map and tested whether the corresponding signatures contributed to socially compliant response formation. NeuroCogMap-guided intervention reduced the sycophancy rate in both models, with lower values indicating better mitigation (Extended Data Fig. 9f). On Gemma2-2B, the sycophancy rate decreased from 28.4% to 26.4% in the Answer setting and from 59.1% to 57.3% in the Feedback setting. On Gemma2-9B-IT, the corresponding reductions were larger, from 20.2% to 18.0% for Answer and from 63.2% to 60.9% for Feedback. Thus, unlike refusal failure, where intervention primarily restored specific refusal-control pathway, sycophancy showed more modest but consistent reduction, consistent with the broader and more distributed weakening of independent control revealed by the preceding analyses. Together, the refusal-failure and sycophancy analyses indicate that behavioural-control pathologies in LLMs are structured failures of output regulation rather than losses of general competence. Refusal failure released procedural action-generation pathways when harmful instructions should have been inhibited, whereas sycophancy weakened conflict monitoring, normative evaluation and self-corrective judgment under social pressure. Because these control signatures were predictive and actionable under parcel steering, NeuroCogMap links systems-level interpretation to mechanism-guided behavioural control.

sections.title: Pathology datasets and representative task patterns
sections.content: The main and extended pathology analyses used four pathology contrasts. Hallucination and social bias were treated as representational pathologies, because the pathological response depends on distorted factual, evidential or demographic representations. Refusal failure and sycophancy were treated as behavioural-control pathologies, because the pathological response reflects failure to regulate output under harmful instructions, safety constraints or social pressure. The datasets were selected to separate superficially similar failures into mechanistically distinct regimes. Hallucination datasets. TruthfulQA and NQ-Open were used as complementary hallucination probes. TruthfulQA tests whether model can suppress salient but false misconception-based answers, whereas NQ-Open tests whether model can coordinate factual retrieval and answer selection for direct open-domain questions.

sections.title: Pathology detection baselines
sections.content: This section describes the baseline detectors used to contextualize NeuroCogMap-based pathology detection in the hallucination, refusal-failure, sycophancy and social-bias analyses. The baselines were chosen to cover three broad comparison families: output-distribution uncertainty, response-consistency measures and generic internal-state or attention probes. Unless otherwise stated, baseline detectors were evaluated on the same normative versus pathological labels used by NeuroCogMap and using the same per-dataset, per-model stratified fivefold cross-validation splits.

sections.title: Shared evaluation protocol
sections.content: For each pathology family, the detection task was formulated as binary classification between normative condition and pathological condition. Hallucination detection separated truthful or reference-supported responses from hallucinated or unsupported responses. Refusal-failure detection separated successful refusal from harmful compliance. Sycophancy detection separated independent responses from user-influenced sycophantic responses. Social-bias detection separated unbiased or fair responses from biased responses. Baselines that produced scalar scores, such as entropy, perplexity or consistency scores, were evaluated as continuous detectors using held-out AUROC. Baselines that produced feature vectors, such as attention features, hidden-state features or early-logit features, used lightweight classifier fit within each training fold and were evaluated from held-out predicted probabilities. This protocol ensured that differences between NeuroCogMap and the baselines reflected the information source used for detection rather than differences in labels or evaluation splits.

sections.title: Hallucination detection baselines
sections.content: We compared NeuroCogMap with six hallucination-detection baselines spanning token-level uncertainty, sequence likelihood, semantic uncertainty, self-consistency and representation-level probing. These baselines test whether hallucination can be detected from output probability distributions, sampled response variability or generic hidden representations, without using the structured parcel, capability and circuit-level organization provided by NeuroCogMap. This comparison follows prior work treating hallucination detection as problem of uncertainty estimation, selective generation, self-consistency assessment and hidden-state separability [68, 35, 36, 37, 33, 34]. Entropy. Entropy is token-level uncertainty baseline [35]. For each generated token, we computed the entropy of the models next-token distribution, 𝐻𝑡 = 𝑣 𝑝(𝑣 𝑥, 𝑦<𝑡) log 𝑝(𝑣 𝑥, 𝑦<𝑡), where 𝑥 is the prompt, 𝑦<𝑡 is the generated prefix and is the vocabulary. The response-level uncertainty score was the aggregate token entropy across the generated answer, 𝐻sum( 𝑦 𝑥) = 𝑇 𝑡=1 𝐻𝑡. Higher entropy indicates that the model distributes probability mass more broadly over possible next tokens across the response. This provides simple white-box uncertainty baseline, but it is affected by answer length and does not directly test whether the generated content is factually correct.

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: LN Entropy. LN Entropy (length-normalized entropy) controls for the dependence of aggregate uncertainty on answer length [35]. For response of 𝑇 generated tokens, the baseline used average token entropy, 𝐻LN ( 𝑦 𝑥) = 1 𝑇 log 𝑇 𝑡=1 𝐻𝑡. This value normalizes aggregate token entropy by response length and by the maximum possible vocabulary entropy, log . LN Entropy therefore measures bounded average predictive uncertainty per generated token, rather than total uncertainty accumulated over the full answer. It remains surface-level uncertainty measure and can miss high-confidence hallucinations. Perplexity. Perplexity measures the likelihood assigned by the evaluated model to the generated response [36]. For each prompt-response pair, we computed the average negative log-likelihood of the generated answer tokens and exponentiated it, (cid:32) PPL( 𝑦 𝑥) = exp (cid:33) log 𝑝( 𝑦𝑡 𝑥, 𝑦<𝑡) . 1 𝑇 𝑇 𝑡=1 Higher perplexity indicates that the generated sequence is less probable under the model. In hallucination detection, this baseline tests whether unsupported answers are also lower-likelihood generations. Its limitation is that fluent hallucinations can remain highly probable when they follow common linguistic or factual patterns. Semantic Entropy. Semantic Entropy estimates uncertainty at the level of meaning rather than individual tokens [37]. Multiple responses were sampled for the same prompt and grouped into semantic equivalence classes. The semantic entropy score was computed over the empirical distribution of semantic clusters, 𝐻sem(𝑥) = 𝑐 𝑝(𝑐 𝑥) log 𝑝(𝑐 𝑥), where denotes the set of semantic clusters. high score indicates that repeated generations produce semantically diverse or mutually inconsistent answers. This baseline is stronger than token entropy when paraphrases express the same answer, but it requires multiple generations and semantic clustering for each prompt. SelfCheckGPT. SelfCheckGPT is black-box self-consistency baseline [33]. For each prompt, multiple sampled responses were generated from the same model and compared with the original response. The core assumption is that factual content supported by the models internal knowledge should be reproduced consistently across samples, whereas hallucinated content is more likely to vary or contradict alternative generations. The hallucination score was derived from inconsistency between the original response and the sampled responses. SelfCheckGPT does not require logits or hidden states, but it is generation-expensive and can fail when model consistently repeats the same incorrect answer. Hidden Probing. Hidden Probing tests whether hallucination labels are decodable from generic internal activations [34]. For each prompt-response pair, hidden states from selected model layers were aggregated into response-level representation, such as an answer-token average or final-token representation. lightweight classifier was then trained to predict the hallucination label, ˆ𝑧 = 𝑔(ℎ), where ℎ denotes the extracted hidden representation and ˆ𝑧 is the predicted probability of hallucination. This baseline uses internal model information rather than output probabilities, but it does not identify which functional parcels, capabilities or circuit-level relations contribute to the pathological state.

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: Refusal-failure detection baselines Refusal-failure baselines were selected to test whether harmful compliance can be detected from prompt likelihood, early output distributions or perturbation sensitivity. The normative class comprised successful refusal, whereas the pathological class comprised refusal failure or jailbreak success. These baselines are related to prior work on adversarial prompting and jailbreak defense in aligned language models [70, 41, 42, 40]. Logits SVM. Logits SVM follows single-pass jailbreak-detection strategy [41]. For each input prompt, single forward pass was used to extract the next-token probabilities associated with the first few output-token positions. If 𝑞𝑡,𝑣 = 𝑝(𝑣 𝑥, 𝑦<𝑡) denotes the probability assigned to vocabulary item 𝑣 at early output position 𝑡, the feature for each vocabulary item was aggregated over the first 𝐾 positions, 𝑧𝑣 = 𝐾 𝑡= 𝑞𝑡,𝑣. The resulting vector 𝑧 was used to train an RBF-SVM classifier, ˆ𝑟 = 𝕀{ 𝑓SVM(𝑧) > 0}, where ˆ𝑟 = 1 denotes predicted refusal failure. The rationale is that unsafe or jailbreak-inducing prompts may alter the models earliest generation distribution before the full harmful answer is produced. This baseline is computationally efficient because it does not require repeated generations, but it treats refusal failure as separability problem in the early output distribution rather than as structured breakdown of internal control organization. SmoothLLM. SmoothLLM is perturbation-robustness baseline for adversarial prompts [42]. For each prompt 𝑥, randomized perturbations 𝑥 (1) , . . . , 𝑥 (𝑀 ) were generated and the model responses 𝑦 (1) , . . . , 𝑦 (𝑀 ) were compared. Response stability was summarized by the dominant-cluster consistency score, 𝐶(𝑥) = 1 𝑀 max 𝑐 {𝑚 : 𝑦 (𝑚) 𝑐}, or equivalently by the entropy of the response-cluster distribution, 𝐻pert (𝑥) = 𝑐 𝑝(𝑐 𝑥) log 𝑝(𝑐 𝑥). Benign prompts are expected to produce relatively stable outputs under small perturbations, whereas adversarial prompts are expected to be less stable. SmoothLLM tests different source of evidence from NeuroCogMap: it detects prompt sensitivity rather than the internal configuration of safety, refusal and action-generation mechanisms. Perplexity. Perplexity was used as likelihood-based refusal-failure baseline [40]. For prompt or promptresponse sequence 𝑠 = (𝑠1, . . . , 𝑠𝑇 ), the baseline computed 𝑇 (cid:33) (cid:32) PPL(𝑠) = exp log 𝑝(𝑠𝑡 𝑠<𝑡) . 1 𝑇 𝑡= High-perplexity examples were treated as more likely to be adversarial or pathological. This baseline is motivated by the observation that some jailbreak prompts are distributionally unusual or unnatural. However, perplexity can miss fluent harmful instructions and can also assign high scores to benign but rare prompts.

sections.title: Sycophancy detection baselines
sections.content: Sycophancy is comparatively recent detection setting, and there is less consensus on standard detectors than in hallucination or jailbreak detection. We therefore constructed baselines from existing sycophancy analyses and related consistency-based detection methods [71, 96, 92, 33]. The normative class comprised independent responses, whereas the pathological class comprised responses that shifted toward the users stated belief, preference or self-presentation.

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: Self-Consistency. Self-Consistency tests whether the model maintains stable answer when user-pressure information is not treated as decisive evidence [33, 96]. For each sycophancy item, repeated sampled responses 𝑦 (1) , . . . , 𝑦 (𝑀 ) were compared under the same user-conditioned prompt. Outputs were grouped according to semantic similarity, producing clusters C. The self-consistency score was summarized as 𝐶self (𝑥) = 1 𝑀 max 𝑐 {𝑚 : 𝑦 (𝑚) 𝑐}, with an equivalent instability score given by cluster entropy, 𝐻self (𝑥) = 𝑐 𝑝(𝑐 𝑥) log 𝑝(𝑐 𝑥). Low consistency or high semantic dispersion indicates that the models answer is unstable under the userconditioned setting. This baseline adapts the self-consistency intuition used in hallucination detection to sycophancy, where the relevant failure is not factual unsupportedness alone, but susceptibility to user-guided response shifts. User-Conditioned Attention. User-Conditioned Attention tests whether sycophancy is associated with excessive attention to user-belief or user-opinion spans [92]. For each prompt, user-conditioning cues were identified, such as statements indicating that the user believes an answer, likes an item, dislikes an item, wrote an item or suggests an alternative answer. If 𝑈 denotes cue-token positions and 𝐴ℓ,𝑡,𝑢 denotes attention from generated answer token 𝑡 to cue token 𝑢 in layer ℓ, the cue-attention score was computed as 𝐴cue = 1 L𝑇 𝑈 𝑇 ℓ 𝑡=1 𝑢𝑈 𝐴ℓ,𝑡,𝑢. Additional features included maximum cue attention and last-token-to-cue attention, and lightweight classifier used these features to distinguish independent from sycophantic responses. This baseline directly tests plausible local mechanism for sycophancy, namely over-weighting the users expressed stance, but it does not model broader circuit-level or capability-level control changes. Perplexity. Perplexity was used as likelihood-based sycophancy baseline. In the answer setting, the models likelihood was computed for the correct answer 𝑦+, the user-suggested incorrect answer 𝑦 and the generated model answer 𝑦 under the user-conditioned prompt 𝑥𝑢. The baseline feature vector was 𝜙PPL (𝑥𝑢) = (cid:2)PPL( 𝑦+ 𝑥𝑢) PPL( 𝑦 𝑥𝑢), PPL( 𝑦 𝑥𝑢), 𝑃, PPL( 𝑦 𝑥𝑢) 𝑃(cid:3) , 2 {PPL( 𝑦+ 𝑥𝑢) + PPL( 𝑦 𝑥𝑢)}. In the feedback setting, each item also included an unguided neutral where 𝑃 = 1 response 𝑦0, generated without the user-conditioning cue. Sycophancy labels were assigned by comparing the user-conditioned evaluative response 𝑦 with this neutral response. The likelihood baseline therefore used the neutral response as the reference and computed features from PPL( 𝑦 𝑥𝑢), PPL( 𝑦0 𝑥𝑢), their absolute difference and their signed difference. This baseline tests whether sycophantic responses are distinguished by surface likelihood under user-conditioned prompts, but it cannot directly separate social compliance from legitimate evidence-based answer revision. Social-bias detection baselines Social-bias baselines were selected to test whether biased responses can be detected from demographic-attention patterns, generic hidden-state separability or likelihood asymmetries among stereotype-relevant answer options. The normative class comprised unbiased, fair or uncertainty-preserving responses, whereas the pathological class comprised biased responses. This comparison is motivated by prior work measuring stereotypical bias and fairness failures in language models [97, 69, 98, 99].

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: Attention Probing. Attention Probing measures whether biased responses are associated with abnormal attention to demographic or protected-attribute spans [98]. For each social-bias item, target and counterfactual demographic attributes were identified in the context. During answer generation, the baseline computed attention from answer tokens to these attribute spans. If 𝑈tar and 𝑈ctr denote target and counter-attribute token positions, their average attention scores were 𝐴tar = 1 L𝑇 𝑈tar 𝑇 ℓ 𝑡=1 𝑢𝑈tar 𝐴ℓ,𝑡,𝑢, 𝐴ctr = 1 L𝑇 𝑈ctr 𝑇 ℓ 𝑡=1 𝑢𝑈ctr 𝐴ℓ,𝑡,𝑢. The feature vector included 𝐴tar, 𝐴ctr and 𝐴tar 𝐴ctr. logistic-regression classifier was then trained to predict whether the response was biased. This baseline tests whether social bias is locally reflected in attention allocation to demographic information, but attention features alone do not establish how demographic information interacts with downstream reasoning or answer selection. Hidden Probing. Hidden Probing uses generic model hidden states to detect social-bias labels [99]. For each prompt-response pair, hidden states from selected layers were aggregated into response-level representations, with middle and late layer features retained to capture both intermediate and output-proximal information. If ℎmid and ℎlate denote the aggregated middleand late-layer states, the probe used ℎ = [ℎmid; ℎlate], ˆ𝑏 = 𝜎(𝑤ℎ + 𝛽), where ˆ𝑏 is the predicted probability of biased response. This baseline asks whether the bias label is decodable from internal states, but it does not explain which functional parcels or capabilities are responsible for the biased response. Perplexity. Perplexity was used as likelihood-based social-bias baseline [97]. For each multiple-choice social-bias item, uncertainty or abstention options were removed when necessary, leaving the substantive stereotype-relevant alternatives 𝑜1 and 𝑜2. This removal was used only to construct likelihood-asymmetry features between stereotype-relevant alternatives; response labels and AUROC evaluation retained the original fair or unbiased versus biased labels, including uncertainty-preserving responses. The model likelihood was computed for each substantive answer option and for the models actual answer 𝑦 under the question context 𝑥. The detector used 𝜙bias(𝑥) = (cid:2)PPL(𝑜1 𝑥) PPL(𝑜2 𝑥), PPL( 𝑦 𝑥), 𝑃𝑜, PPL( 𝑦 𝑥) 𝑃𝑜 (cid:3) , where 𝑃𝑜 = 1 2 {PPL(𝑜1 𝑥) + PPL(𝑜2 𝑥)}. This baseline tests whether biased responses are accompanied by likelihood asymmetries among stereotype-relevant alternatives, but it does not identify the representational mechanism by which demographic information influences inference.

sections.title: Comparison with NeuroCogMap
sections.content: The baselines above provide complementary but relatively coarse evidence about pathological responses. Entropy, LN Entropy and Perplexity quantify uncertainty or likelihood in the models output distribution. Semantic Entropy, SelfCheckGPT, SmoothLLM and Self-Consistency quantify instability across sampled or perturbed responses. Logits SVM, Attention Probing, User-Conditioned Attention and Hidden Probing use early logits, attention features or generic hidden states to test whether pathology labels are decodable from local or unstructured internal signals. NeuroCogMap differs from these baselines by representing each response through structured deviations in parcel activation, capability recruitment and circuit connectivity. It therefore uses the same multilevel cognitive organization that supports interpretation to construct the detector. This makes NeuroCogMap stronger mechanistic comparison point: it tests whether pathology is better detected as coordinated disruption of internal cognitive organization than as generic change in uncertainty, likelihood, response instability, attention to salient tokens or hidden-state separability.

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: Figure 10 ac, Pathology detection from NeuroCogMap-derived signatures in Llama-3.1-8B, measured by AUROC under the same normative-versus-pathological labels used in the main Gemma2 analyses. Hallucination detection was evaluated on NQ-Open and TruthfulQA against Entropy, LN Entropy (length-normalized entropy), Perplexity, Semantic Entropy, SelfCheckGPT and Hidden Probing (a); refusal-failure detection was evaluated on AdvBench and JBB-Behaviors against Logits SVM, SmoothLLM and Perplexity (b); and sycophancy detection was evaluated on the Answer and Feedback tracks against Self-Consistency, User-Conditioned Attention and Perplexity (c). dg, NeuroCogMap-guided parcel intervention in Llama-3.1-8B across hallucination (d), social bias (e), refusal failure (f) and sycophancy (g). Hallucination is measured by answer accuracy on MedHallu, NQ-Open and TruthfulQA; social bias by fairness accuracy, defined as the unbiased-response rate on BBQ subsets; refusal failure by refusal accuracy on JBB-Behaviors and AdvBench; and sycophancy by sycophancy rate, where lower values indicate improvement. Social-bias detection results are not plotted because AUROC values were close to ceiling across BBQ subsets. Bars show cross-validation means for detection and dataset-level outcomes for intervention; error bars indicate standard deviation where available. Cross-model safety-pathology detection and intervention in Llama-3.1-8B To test whether NeuroCogMap-derived pathology signatures were restricted to the Gemma2 model family, we repeated the safety-pathology detection and intervention analyses in Llama-3.1-8B [25]. The analysis used the same normative-versus-pathological labels, pathology families and baseline definitions as the main Gemma2 experiments. Detection was evaluated by AUROC, and intervention was evaluated by the task-level behavioural outcome used for each pathology family (Extended Data Fig. 10). In hallucination detection, NeuroCogMap showed the strongest AUROC among the plotted methods in Llama3.1-8B on both datasets. On NQ-Open, NeuroCogMap achieved an AUROC of 0.691, exceeding SelfCheckGPT (0.673), Perplexity (0.638), Hidden Probing (0.632), Entropy (0.625), LN Entropy (0.586), and Semantic Entropy (0.580). On TruthfulQA, NeuroCogMap also ranked highest, with an AUROC of 0.663, outperforming Hidden Probing (0.632), Perplexity (0.624), Entropy (0.570), Semantic Entropy (0.549), LN Entropy (0.548), and SelfCheckGPT (0.496). Thus, hallucination detection in Llama-3.1-8B showed consistent NeuroCogMap signature across both open-domain factual answering and truthfulness-oriented evaluation, with the largest margin observed on TruthfulQA. Refusal-failure detection showed stronger cross-model generalization. NeuroCogMap achieved AUROC values of 0.984 on AdvBench and 0.953 on JBB-Behaviors, exceeding Perplexity (0.852 and 0.842), Logits SVM (0.486 and 0.570) and SmoothLLM (0.485 and 0.521). These results indicate that refusal failure in Llama-3.1-8B was more clearly detected from structured NeuroCogMap signatures than from prompt likelihood, early-output logits or perturbation sensitivity. Sycophancy detection was more modest. NeuroCogMap reached AUROC values of 0.642 on the Answer track and 0.660 on the Feedback track. These values exceeded Perplexity (0.567 and 0.539) and Self-Consistency (0.519 and 0.402), and were comparable to User-Conditioned Attention (0.675 and 0.657). This pattern suggests that sycophancy in Llama-3.1-8B is partially reflected in structured NeuroCogMap pathology signatures, but also remains decodable from user-conditioned local signals. Social-bias detection was not included in the plotted detection panel because all methods showed limited discriminative headroom under near-ceiling separability across BBQ subsets. NeuroCogMap reached AUROC values of 1.00 on BBQ-Age, BBQ-Nationality, BBQ-Gender and BBQ-Disability. This may reflect highly sep-

sections.title: NeuroCogMap Reveals Cognitive Organization of Large Language Models
sections.content: arable response-state structure in these BBQ subsets for Llama-3.1-8B; we therefore report these values as supplementary evidence rather than using them as main visual comparison. NeuroCogMap-guided parcel intervention produced directionally consistent improvements across most Llama-3.1-8B pathology settings. For hallucination, answer accuracy increased from 78.6% to 84.7% on MedHallu, from 42.9% to 46.1% on NQ-Open and from 26.9% to 29.9% on TruthfulQA. For social bias, intervention produced heterogeneous effects, with small decrease on BBQ-Age (69.2% to 68.8%), minimal change on BBQ-Nationality (71.5% to 71.6%), and improvements on BBQ-Gender (70.6% to 72.8%) and BBQ-Disability (50.1% to 54.3%). For refusal failure, refusal accuracy increased from 23.2% to 30.2% on JBB-Behaviors and from 72.4% to 79.8% on AdvBench. For sycophancy, the sycophancy rate decreased from 26.4% to 25.3% on the Answer track and from 64.4% to 61.9% on the Feedback track. Together, these Llama-3.1-8B analyses indicate that NeuroCogMap-derived pathology signatures are not restricted to the Gemma2 model family. The strongest cross-model generalization was observed for refusalfailure detection and hallucination/refusal intervention, whereas social-bias detection showed near-ceiling separability and sycophancy intervention produced smaller but directionally consistent changes. sections.title: Parcel construction is stable across clustering hyperparameters
sections.content: We examined whether the NeuroCogMap parcellation was sensitive to clustering hyperparameters by repeating parcel construction on Gemma2-2B across 16 settings defined by four SVD variance-retention thresholds (75%, 80%, 85% and 90%) and four numbers of clustering iterations (30, 50, 70 and 100). Similarity between two parcellation solutions was quantified using the symmetric best-match Jaccard score, which accounts for the fact that parcel labels are not naturally aligned across separately constructed parcellation solutions. Across all off-diagonal pairs of parcellation solutions, parcel assignments were highly consistent (mean similarity, 0.786; median similarity, 0.725; range, 0.6421.000; Supplementary Table 4). This stability was especially strong when the SVD variance-retention threshold was fixed and only the number of clustering iterations varied. Within-threshold similarities were nearly identical across iteration settings (mean similarity, 0.999; median similarity, 1.000; range, 0.9951.000), indicating that the clustering procedure had effectively converged within the tested iteration range. By contrast, variation in the SVD variance-retention threshold produced structured, rather than random, change in parcel composition. The lowest similarities occurred when the 75% retention setting was compared with higher-retention solutions (75% versus 80%: mean similarity, 0.663; 75% versus 85%: 0.642; 75% versus 90%: 0.642), indicating that aggressive dimensionality reduction altered part of the parcel structure. Similarity increased when more latent variance was retained: the 80% solution showed stronger agreement with the 85% and 90% solutions (both mean similarity, 0.725), whereas the 85% and 90% solutions were almost indistinguishable (mean similarity, 1.000; range, 0.9991.000). Thus, parcel construction was insensitive to the number of optimization iterations and converged to highly stable assignments once sufficient latent variance was retained. To assess whether these similarities exceeded chance, we compared each observed solution pair against size-preserving permutation null in which latent assignments were randomly reassigned while keeping parcel sizes fixed. All off-diagonal solution pairs were significantly more similar than the corresponding null distribution (all one-sided permutation tests, 𝑃 0.001, 1,000 permutations). Together, these results indicate that NeuroCogMap parcel construction is robust to clustering-iteration settings and shows stable high-retention regime across SVD thresholds, supporting the reproducibility of the functional atlas. Capability-associated parcels show hierarchical semantic and structural organization Fig. 2d shows that the NeuroCogMap hierarchy is supported by both semantic coherence among hierarchylinked parcels and functional dependency from lower-level support processes to higher-level reasoning. Related work in cognitive and network neuroscience has examined hierarchical organization in terms of both functional diversity and the structural position of components within broader system. These analyses were performed on the Gemma2-2B NeuroCogMap atlas and repeated in the Llama-3.1-8B atlas as an independent cross-model replication. First, we quantified the semantic dispersion of parcels associated with each cognitive level (Extended Data Fig. 11a,b). For each capability, we selected its highest-ranked associated parcels and represented each parcel by the concatenation of its functional name and description. Parcel descriptions were embedded with Qwen3-Embedding-8B, and semantic dispersion was defined as one minus the mean pairwise cosine similarity among parcel descriptions within hierarchy level. To assess robustness to the number of parcels retained per capability, the analysis was repeated after retaining the top 5, 10, 15, 20 or 25 parcels. Semantic dispersion increased along the cognitive hierarchy in Gemma2-2B, from perceptual access and attentional gating to situated application and social interaction (0.406 0.011 to 0.466 0.007; linear trend 𝑅2 = 0.877). The same overall pattern was observed in Llama-3.1-8B (0.400 0.003 to 0.454 0.004; 𝑅2 = 0.857). Thus, higher-level capabilities were associated with parcel sets that were less mutually similar and occupied broader functional space, consistent with the idea that abstraction and situated application require the coordination of more heterogeneous component operations. Second, we examined whether hierarchy-linked parcels differed in their structural position based on the model-specific structural connectome (Extended Data Fig. 11c,d). sections.content: All off-diagonal pairs 120 0.786 0.725 Same variance-retention threshold, different iteration numbers Different variance-retention thresholds 75% versus higher-retention thresholds 80% versus 85%/90% retention thresholds 85% versus 90% retention thresholds Size-preserving permutation null 24 96 48 16 0.999 1.000 0.733 0.694 0. 0.642 0.725 0.725 1.000 1.000 0.642 1.000 0.995 1.000 0.642 1.000 0.642 0. 0.724 0.726 0.999 1.000 Parcel assignments were highly consistent across all tested hyperparameter settings. Changing the number of clustering iterations had little effect on parcel assignments. Most variability arose from changing the amount of retained latent variance rather than from iteration number. The 75% retention setting showed the lowest agreement with higher-retention solutions. Similarity increased when more latent structure was retained. High-retention settings yielded nearly identical parcellations. All off-diagonal solution pairs were significantly more similar than the corresponding random controls (all 𝑃 0.001; one-sided permutation tests, 1,000 permutations). Similarity was computed as the symmetric best-match Jaccard score [100] between two parcellation solutions. The 16 solutions were generated from four SVD variance-retention thresholds (75%, 80%, 85% and 90%) and four clustering-iteration settings (30, 50, 70 and 100). For the permutation null, latent assignments were randomly reassigned while preserving parcel sizes. The reported 𝑃 value is the empirical one-sided probability 𝑃 = Pr(𝑆null 𝑆obs ). directed parcelparcel connectivity matrix was converted into an undirected, unweighted graph by retaining the strongest 10% of off-diagonal absolute weights; because the underlying connectome is directed, an undirected edge was counted if either direction exceeded the threshold. Parcel degree was defined as the number of neighbours in this thresholded graph. Each capability contributed its ten highest-ranked parcels; duplicate parcels were collapsed within each hierarchy level and ranked by recurrence across capabilities, and robustness was assessed by retaining 10, 15, 20, 25 or 30 representative parcels per level. Mean degree decreased across the hierarchy in Gemma2-2B, from 62.71 4.10 for perceptual access and attentional gating to 34.84 3.40 for situated application and social interaction (𝑅2 = 0.921). The decrease was replicated in Llama-3.1-8B, from 66.64 2.53 to 31.95 1.47 (𝑅2 = 0.996). Lower-level capability-associated parcels therefore occupied more connected positions than higher-level parcels within the model-level structural connectome. Together, these analyses link the semantic and structural organization of hierarchy-associated parcels (Extended Data Fig. 11). Higher levels were associated with broader parcel semantics, consistent with the integration of multiple component operations, whereas lower levels occupied more connected positions in the structural connectome, consistent with reusable access and representational support. This division of labour complements the dependency results in Fig. 2d and suggests hierarchical architecture in which functionally diverse higher-order cognition is supported by broadly connected lower-level substrate. The same organization echoes broader cognitive-neuroscience view in which complex cognition emerges through the coordinated reuse of representational resources within structured networks.

People born on January 7, 2022 include Carl Parker Hedinger and Jamie Katrina Farrier. So the answer is Jamie Katrina Farrier Two-hop context QA. Carl Parker Hedinger was born on January 7, 2022. Held-out generation example. Who was born on the same day as Vivian Arianna Klute? Answer: Kelly Harriet Heaberlin During final evaluation, the model received the available context when present, followed by the question and Answer:. Generation used temperature 0, top-𝑝 = 1 and maximum length 50, and prediction was counted as correct if the generated text contained the gold answer string. Psych-101 cognitive-task datasets used in Fig. 6b Fig. 6b tested whether NeuroCogMap activations predict variation in the behavioural fit between language model and individual human participants across distinct cognitive domains. We analysed five participant-level datasets from Psych-101 [58], spanning episodic long-term memory, multi-attribute decision-making, Shepard categorization, intertemporal choice and drifting four-armed bandit learning. As described in Methods, each participant history was converted into behavioural context, Gemma2-2B was evaluated on the likelihood assigned to the observed human responses, and participant-level NeuroCogMap activation vectors were used to predict held-out behavioural fit. The details below specify the task structure, participant counts and behavioural sequences used in this analysis. Episodic long-term memory. The episodic long-term memory dataset was taken from the intentional versus incidental learning experiment of Popov and Dames [62]. Participants completed repeated cycles containing word-study phase, an arithmetic distractor phase and recall phase. During study, each word was presented with coloured border indicating whether it should be intentionally remembered, while participants also made semantic judgement about whether the object denoted by the word was larger or smaller than football. The analysed behavioural record therefore combined encoding decisions and later recall responses. Representative items included words such as church, robot and jewelry; model fit reflected the likelihood assigned to observed semantic judgements and recall outputs in the context of the preceding study history.
